name: Bethanechol
moa: Selective muscarinic agonist resistant to acetylcholinesterase; activates M3 receptors on bladder detrusor and GI smooth muscle to stimulate motility/voiding
indications: Postoperative and neurogenic urinary retention; postoperative ileus and GI atony (no obstruction)
side_effects: Cholinergic excess (SLUDGE): salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation, GI cramping/diarrhea, emesis; flushing
pearl: "Bethany, call (bethanechol) me to activate your bowels and bladder." No nicotinic activity; contraindicated in asthma/COPD, peptic ulcer, and bladder/bowel obstruction.

name: Carbachol
moa: Activates muscarinic and nicotinic receptors and is resistant to AChE; in the eye causes miosis and increases aqueous humor outflow to lower IOP
indications: Glaucoma; induces miosis intraoperatively during ocular surgery
side_effects: Miosis, brow ache, blurred near vision, ocular irritation; systemic SLUDGE if absorbed
pearl: Poorly hydrolyzed by AChE, so long-acting; topical ocular agent, a second-line miotic/IOP-lowering drug when pilocarpine is inadequate.

name: Pilocarpine
drug_class: Direct-acting muscarinic agonist (natural alkaloid, tertiary amine)
moa: Stimulates muscarinic receptors; ciliary muscle contraction opens the trabecular meshwork (open-angle) and pupillary sphincter contraction (miosis) opens the angle (angle-closure); stimulates exocrine glands
indications: Acute angle-closure glaucoma (emergency); chronic open-angle glaucoma; xerostomia (Sjogren, post head/neck radiation)
side_effects: Profuse sweating and salivation, miosis, brow ache, blurred vision; bronchospasm
pearl: "You cry, drool, and sweat with pilocarpine." Tertiary amine; potent stimulator of sweat and other exocrine glands; especially used for emergency miosis in angle-closure.

name: Methacholine
moa: Muscarinic agonist that provokes bronchoconstriction; only partially hydrolyzed by AChE so longer-acting than ACh
indications: Diagnostic only: methacholine challenge test for bronchial hyperreactivity (suspected asthma)
side_effects: Bronchospasm, dyspnea, wheezing, cough (the intended diagnostic effect)
pearl: Positive test = >=20% drop in FEV1 from baseline; reverse with inhaled albuterol. Not used therapeutically; avoid in patients with baseline severe airflow obstruction.

name: Neostigmine
moa: Reversibly inhibits acetylcholinesterase, increasing ACh at synapses; quaternary charge prevents CNS penetration
indications: Reversal of nondepolarizing neuromuscular blockade; myasthenia gravis; postoperative ileus and urinary retention
side_effects: SLUDGE, bradycardia, bronchospasm, miosis; cholinergic crisis (weakness, fasciculations) in overdose
pearl: "Neo CNS = No CNS." Co-administer glycopyrrolate or atropine to block muscarinic effects when reversing paralysis.

name: Pyridostigmine
moa: Reversibly inhibits AChE, increasing ACh at the NMJ; longer duration of action than neostigmine; no CNS entry
indications: First-line chronic maintenance therapy for myasthenia gravis; pretreatment prophylaxis before soman (nerve gas) exposure
side_effects: SLUDGE/diarrhea, abdominal cramps, increased salivation, muscle fasciculations
pearl: Long duration makes it the chronic MG drug; often given with glycopyrrolate/propantheline to limit GI muscarinic effects. Pretreatment (not antidote) for soman.

name: Edrophonium
moa: Briefly and reversibly inhibits AChE; rapid onset (seconds) and short duration (~5-10 min)
indications: Historically diagnosed MG and distinguished myasthenic from cholinergic crisis (Tensilon test)
side_effects: Bradycardia, hypotension, increased secretions; can transiently worsen weakness in cholinergic crisis
pearl: Myasthenic weakness improves with edrophonium; cholinergic-crisis weakness worsens. Largely replaced by anti-AChR antibody testing.

name: Physostigmine
moa: Reversibly inhibits AChE; as a tertiary amine it crosses the blood-brain barrier to raise central and peripheral ACh
indications: Antidote for antimuscarinic/anticholinergic toxicity (e.g., atropine, scopolamine, antihistamines, jimsonweed)
side_effects: Cholinergic excess: bradycardia, bronchospasm, seizures; asystole/bradyasystole in overdose
pearl: "Phys is physostigmine for the physician's brain" - CNS-penetrant antidote that reverses central anticholinergic delirium. Avoid in TCA overdose (seizures, asystole).

name: Donepezil
moa: Reversibly inhibits central acetylcholinesterase, raising ACh in cortex and hippocampus
indications: Symptomatic treatment of mild-to-severe Alzheimer disease
side_effects: Nausea, diarrhea, insomnia/vivid dreams, bradycardia, syncope, muscle cramps
pearl: Once-daily dosing; improves symptoms but does not slow disease progression. Rivastigmine and galantamine are same-class alternatives.

name: Rivastigmine
moa: Inhibits both acetylcholinesterase and butyrylcholinesterase centrally to increase ACh
indications: Alzheimer disease; dementia associated with Parkinson disease
side_effects: Prominent GI: nausea, vomiting, anorexia, weight loss; bradycardia
pearl: Available as a transdermal patch to reduce GI side effects; notable for use in Parkinson-disease dementia.

name: Galantamine
drug_class: Centrally-acting reversible AChE inhibitor (+ nicotinic modulator)
moa: Reversibly inhibits AChE and allosterically potentiates nicotinic ACh receptors
indications: Mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease
side_effects: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, weight loss, bradycardia
pearl: Dual mechanism (AChE inhibition plus nicotinic allosteric modulation); derived from snowdrop/daffodil alkaloids.

name: Tacrine
moa: Reversibly inhibits central acetylcholinesterase to boost ACh
indications: Historically used for Alzheimer disease (now obsolete)
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity (elevated transaminases), nausea, diarrhea
pearl: Abandoned due to hepatotoxicity and 4x/day dosing; classic exam answer for the AChE inhibitor that causes liver damage.

name: Echothiophate
moa: Irreversibly phosphorylates and inactivates acetylcholinesterase, dramatically increasing ACh
indications: Chronic open-angle glaucoma (rarely used today)
side_effects: Intense miosis, brow ache, cataracts, systemic cholinergic toxicity
pearl: Topical ocular organophosphate; long-acting. Depletes plasma pseudocholinesterase, prolonging succinylcholine effect.

name: Pralidoxime (2-PAM)
moa: Regenerates active acetylcholinesterase by cleaving the organophosphate from the enzyme, before "aging" occurs
indications: Antidote for organophosphate (insecticide/nerve agent) poisoning, given together with atropine
side_effects: Tachycardia, transient muscle weakness, blurred vision, hypertension/laryngospasm with rapid infusion
pearl: Give EARLY - once the enzyme "ages" (loses an alkyl group) inhibition is permanent and 2-PAM fails. Atropine treats muscarinic symptoms; pralidoxime reverses nicotinic (muscle) symptoms.

name: Atropine
moa: Competitively blocks muscarinic ACh receptors (M1-M5); tertiary amine, crosses BBB.
indications: Symptomatic bradycardia/AV block, organophosphate & cholinergic (muscarinic) poisoning, pre-op antisialagogue.
side_effects: Mydriasis, cycloplegia/blurred vision, dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, tachycardia, hyperthermia, delirium.
pearl: Toxidrome: 'hot, dry, red, blind, mad.' Antidote is physostigmine; atropine itself is the antidote for organophosphate/cholinergic poisoning.

name: Scopolamine (hyoscine)
moa: Blocks muscarinic receptors; highly CNS-penetrant, acts on vestibular nuclei and cortex.
indications: Motion sickness (transdermal patch), post-op nausea/vomiting, antisialagogue.
side_effects: Sedation, anterograde amnesia, dry mouth, blurred vision, confusion (esp. elderly).
pearl: Best agent for motion sickness; transdermal patch behind ear. Central effects predominate over peripheral.

name: Ipratropium
moa: Blocks M3 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle, causing bronchodilation; quaternary, so poor systemic/CNS absorption.
indications: COPD (maintenance & exacerbations), acute asthma (adjunct to SABA), rhinorrhea.
side_effects: Dry mouth, cough, throat irritation; minimal systemic effects due to poor absorption.
pearl: Short-acting (SAMA). Quaternary = stays in lung, no CNS effects. Often combined with albuterol.

name: Tiotropium
moa: Blocks M3 (slow dissociation, kinetic selectivity) on airway smooth muscle, giving sustained bronchodilation.
indications: COPD maintenance therapy; add-on for severe/uncontrolled asthma.
side_effects: Dry mouth, urinary retention, blurred vision/angle-closure risk (if eye contact); minimal systemic.
pearl: Long-acting (LAMA), once-daily. Slow M3 dissociation distinguishes it from short-acting ipratropium.

name: Oxybutynin
moa: Blocks M3 on detrusor muscle, reducing involuntary contractions; also direct smooth-muscle antispasmodic.
indications: Overactive bladder, urge incontinence, neurogenic bladder.
side_effects: Prominent dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision, confusion in elderly, tachycardia.
pearl: Crosses BBB to cause CNS/cognitive impairment in elderly. Transdermal/ER forms reduce dry mouth.

name: Tolterodine
moa: Non-selective muscarinic antagonist with functional selectivity for bladder over salivary glands.
indications: Overactive bladder, urge incontinence.
side_effects: Dry mouth (less than oxybutynin), constipation, blurred vision.
pearl: Better tolerated than oxybutynin (less dry mouth) due to functional bladder selectivity.

name: Solifenacin
moa: M3-preferring receptor antagonism on detrusor smooth muscle; long half-life.
indications: Overactive bladder, urge incontinence.
side_effects: Dry mouth, constipation, blurred vision; dose-dependent QT prolongation.
pearl: Tertiary amine still crosses BBB—use caution in elderly. Once-daily long half-life. M3-preferring, not absolutely selective.

name: Benztropine
moa: Central M1 blockade restores dopamine/ACh balance in the striatum.
indications: Parkinson disease (tremor-predominant), drug-induced EPS/acute dystonia.
side_effects: Dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, urinary retention, confusion, sedation.
pearl: First-line for antipsychotic-induced acute dystonia/EPS. Helps tremor & rigidity more than bradykinesia.

name: Trihexyphenidyl
moa: Central muscarinic blockade rebalancing striatal ACh excess relative to dopamine.
indications: Parkinson disease (tremor), drug-induced parkinsonism/EPS.
side_effects: Anticholinergic: dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, confusion in elderly.
pearl: Like benztropine—targets tremor; avoid in elderly due to cognitive/anticholinergic burden.

name: Glycopyrrolate
moa: Blocks peripheral muscarinic receptors; quaternary, so no BBB penetration (no CNS effects).
indications: Pre-op antisialagogue, intraop secretion control, blocks muscarinic effects of neostigmine reversal, chronic sialorrhea.
side_effects: Dry mouth, tachycardia, urinary retention, constipation; no central effects.
pearl: Paired with neostigmine to block muscarinic effects during NMB reversal. No CNS penetration (quaternary).

name: Tropicamide
moa: Blocks M3 on iris sphincter and ciliary muscle to produce mydriasis + cycloplegia.
indications: Diagnostic mydriasis/cycloplegia for fundoscopic exams.
side_effects: Blurred vision, photophobia, stinging; can precipitate acute angle-closure glaucoma.
pearl: Short-acting mydriatic of choice for eye exams. Avoid in narrow-angle glaucoma.

name: Cyclopentolate
moa: Antagonizes muscarinic receptors of iris/ciliary body for mydriasis and cycloplegia.
indications: Cycloplegic refraction (esp. children), uveitis (prevents synechiae).
side_effects: Photophobia, blurred vision; CNS effects in children (disorientation, hallucinations); angle-closure risk.
pearl: Preferred cycloplegic for pediatric refraction; watch for CNS toxicity in kids.

name: Dicyclomine
moa: Blocks muscarinic receptors on GI smooth muscle plus direct smooth-muscle relaxation, reducing spasm/motility.
indications: Irritable bowel syndrome, functional GI spasm.
side_effects: Dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, urinary retention, dizziness.
pearl: GI antispasmodic for IBS; contraindicated in GI/urinary obstruction and narrow-angle glaucoma.

name: Aclidinium
moa: Long-acting M3 blockade in airway smooth muscle for bronchodilation; rapid plasma hydrolysis limits systemic effects.
indications: COPD maintenance (twice-daily inhaled).
side_effects: Dry mouth, cough, headache; minimal systemic anticholinergic effects.
pearl: LAMA with rapid plasma inactivation for low systemic exposure; twice-daily vs tiotropium once-daily.

name: Epinephrine
drug_class: Nonselective direct agonist (alpha-1, alpha-2, beta-1, beta-2)
moa: Agonist at all adrenergic receptors; low doses favor beta effects, high doses recruit alpha-1 vasoconstriction
indications: Anaphylaxis (drug of choice), cardiac arrest/ACLS, open-angle glaucoma, vasoconstrictor added to local anesthetics
side_effects: Hypertension, tachyarrhythmia, anxiety, tremor, hyperglycemia, risk of cerebral hemorrhage
pearl: Epinephrine reversal: after alpha-blockade, pressor effect flips to net hypotension via unmasked beta-2 vasodilation

name: Norepinephrine
drug_class: Direct agonist (alpha-1 > alpha-2; some beta-1, minimal beta-2)
moa: Potent alpha-1 vasoconstriction raises SVR/BP; baroreceptor reflex can cause bradycardia despite direct beta-1 effect
indications: Septic shock (first-line vasopressor) and other distributive/hypotensive shock states
side_effects: Reflex bradycardia, severe HTN, tissue necrosis on extravasation, organ/digital ischemia
pearl: Extravasation antidote = local phentolamine (alpha-blocker) infiltration to prevent skin necrosis

name: Isoproterenol
moa: Beta-1 raises HR and contractility; beta-2 vasodilation lowers diastolic BP and widens pulse pressure
indications: Refractory bradyarrhythmias/AV block; bridging in torsades; rarely used now
side_effects: Tachycardia, palpitations, arrhythmia, flushing, hypotension, angina
pearl: Classic clue: increases HR with decreased diastolic BP (pure beta, no alpha vasoconstriction)

name: Dopamine
moa: Low dose D1 renal/mesenteric vasodilation; intermediate beta-1 inotropy; high dose alpha-1 vasoconstriction
indications: Cardiogenic and septic shock, symptomatic bradycardia, low cardiac output states
side_effects: Tachyarrhythmias, hypertension at high dose, tissue ischemia, nausea
pearl: Norepinephrine now preferred over dopamine in septic shock (fewer arrhythmias and lower mortality)

name: Dobutamine
moa: Beta-1 stimulation increases contractility (inotropy) more than rate, with little change in BP
indications: Acute decompensated heart failure, cardiogenic shock, pharmacologic stress echocardiography
side_effects: Tachycardia, arrhythmia, increased myocardial O2 demand, can lower BP via beta-2
pearl: Inotrope of choice in acute HF; raises cardiac output with minimal change in systemic vascular resistance

name: Phenylephrine
moa: Direct alpha-1 vasoconstriction raises BP, triggering baroreceptor-mediated reflex (vagal) bradycardia
indications: Hypotension (anesthesia, vagally mediated), nasal decongestant, mydriatic, paroxysmal SVT
side_effects: Reflex bradycardia, HTN, rebound nasal congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa)
pearl: Produces mydriasis WITHOUT cycloplegia (no ciliary muscle effect, so accommodation preserved)

name: Albuterol
moa: Beta-2 stimulation relaxes bronchial smooth muscle, producing bronchodilation
indications: Acute asthma/COPD bronchospasm (rescue inhaler), acute hyperkalemia (shifts K+ intracellularly)
side_effects: Tremor, tachycardia, palpitations, hypokalemia, hyperglycemia
pearl: Rescue inhaler; terbutaline is the beta-2 agonist used as a tocolytic to delay preterm labor

name: Salmeterol
moa: Lipophilic side chain anchors near the beta-2 receptor for prolonged bronchodilation (~12 hr)
indications: Asthma maintenance (only combined with an ICS) and COPD; NOT for acute attacks
side_effects: Tremor, tachycardia, hypokalemia; increased asthma death risk as monotherapy
pearl: LABA monotherapy raises asthma mortality; must be combined with an inhaled corticosteroid

name: Clonidine
moa: Central alpha-2 stimulation decreases sympathetic outflow, lowering NE release, HR, and BP
indications: Hypertension (incl. with renal disease), ADHD, opioid/alcohol withdrawal, Tourette, hot flashes
side_effects: Sedation, dry mouth, bradycardia, severe rebound HTN if abruptly stopped
pearl: Abrupt withdrawal causes rebound hypertensive crisis; does not decrease renal blood flow, so safe in renal disease

name: Methyldopa
moa: Converted to alpha-methylnorepinephrine, a central alpha-2 agonist that reduces sympathetic outflow
indications: Hypertension in pregnancy (preferred, well-established safety)
side_effects: Sedation, positive direct Coombs test, hemolytic anemia, drug-induced lupus, hepatotoxicity
pearl: Antihypertensive of choice in pregnancy; classic cause of Coombs-positive autoimmune hemolytic anemia

name: Amphetamine
moa: Enters the nerve terminal via reuptake transporters, displaces NE/DA/5-HT from vesicles, and reverses transporters to release them
indications: ADHD, narcolepsy
side_effects: HTN, tachycardia, insomnia, anorexia/weight loss, psychosis, dependence, growth suppression in children
pearl: Acts indirectly (requires intact NE stores); effect blunted by reserpine or prior catecholamine depletion

name: Ephedrine
moa: Directly stimulates alpha/beta receptors AND indirectly releases stored NE; crosses BBB as a CNS stimulant
indications: Hypotension (esp. anesthesia/spinal block); historically nasal decongestion and asthma
side_effects: HTN, tachycardia, insomnia, tremor, tachyphylaxis with repeated dosing
pearl: Tachyphylaxis from depleted NE stores; pseudoephedrine is the related OTC decongestant (precursor in meth synthesis)

name: Midodrine
moa: Active metabolite desglymidodrine selectively stimulates peripheral alpha-1 to cause venous/arterial constriction
indications: Orthostatic hypotension (autonomic dysfunction), hepatorenal syndrome
side_effects: Supine hypertension, piloerection, scalp tingling/pruritus, urinary retention
pearl: Hallmark adverse effect is supine hypertension; avoid dosing near bedtime and keep head elevated

name: Dexmedetomidine
moa: Selective alpha-2 agonist acting at the locus coeruleus to produce sedation/analgesia without significant respiratory depression
indications: ICU sedation, procedural sedation, anesthetic adjunct
side_effects: Bradycardia, hypotension (transient HTN with bolus), dry mouth
pearl: Unique: sedation with minimal respiratory depression; patients remain cooperative and arousable

name: Phenoxybenzamine
moa: Irreversibly (covalently) blocks alpha-1 and alpha-2; effect outlasts the drug until new receptors are synthesized (insurmountable antagonism).
indications: Preoperative management of pheochromocytoma to control catecholamine-driven hypertension.
side_effects: Orthostatic hypotension, reflex tachycardia, nasal congestion, miosis, inhibited ejaculation.
pearl: Pheo: alpha-block FIRST (7-14 days), then add beta-blocker for reflex tachycardia. Never beta-block first - risk unopposed alpha vasoconstriction/hypertensive crisis.

name: Phentolamine
moa: Reversibly/competitively blocks alpha-1 and alpha-2 receptors.
indications: Pheochromocytoma (acute/intraop HTN), alpha-agonist (NE) extravasation, MAOI-tyramine hypertensive crisis.
side_effects: Orthostatic hypotension, marked reflex tachycardia/arrhythmia, nasal congestion, GI stimulation.
pearl: Short-acting; antidote for skin necrosis from extravasated norepinephrine/dopamine (local injection). Classic board link to MAOI-tyramine crisis.

name: Prazosin
moa: Competitively blocks alpha-1 causing arteriolar and venous dilation; relaxes prostatic/bladder-neck smooth muscle.
indications: Hypertension, BPH, PTSD-associated nightmares.
side_effects: First-dose orthostatic hypotension/syncope, dizziness, headache, reflex tachycardia.
pearl: Classic 'first-dose phenomenon' - start low, give at bedtime. -zosin suffix. Uniquely useful for PTSD nightmares.

name: Tamsulosin
moa: Preferentially blocks alpha-1A receptors in prostate/bladder neck, relaxing smooth muscle with minimal vascular alpha-1B effect.
indications: BPH symptomatic relief; medical expulsive therapy for ureteral (distal) stones.
side_effects: Abnormal/retrograde ejaculation, dizziness, less orthostatic hypotension than non-selective agents.
pearl: Intraoperative Floppy Iris Syndrome (IFIS) during cataract surgery - warn ophthalmologist. Minimal BP effect makes it BPH-preferred.

name: Doxazosin
moa: Competitive alpha-1 antagonist; arteriolar/venous dilation plus prostatic smooth-muscle relaxation; long half-life.
indications: Hypertension, BPH.
side_effects: Orthostatic hypotension, dizziness, fatigue, edema.
pearl: ALLHAT: doxazosin arm stopped early for ~2x heart failure vs chlorthalidone. Alpha-blockers are NOT first-line for HTN.

name: Tolazoline
moa: Competitive non-selective alpha-1/alpha-2 antagonist with direct vasodilatory and histamine-like (H1/H2) actions.
indications: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN) - historical/niche.
side_effects: Systemic hypotension (non-selective vasodilation), tachyarrhythmia, GI bleeding/stimulation, flushing.
pearl: Imidazoline relative of phentolamine; largely historical. Not a selective pulmonary vasodilator - can drop systemic BP and worsen R-to-L shunt.

name: Yohimbine
moa: Blocks presynaptic alpha-2 autoreceptors, increasing central and peripheral sympathetic (noradrenergic) outflow.
indications: Historically erectile dysfunction; now mainly a pharmacology teaching/research probe.
side_effects: Hypertension, tachycardia, anxiety, tremor, sweating.
pearl: Prototype alpha-2 blocker - functional opposite of clonidine; raises BP/sympathetic tone. Avoid in anxiety disorders and with MAOIs.

name: Mirtazapine
moa: Blocks central alpha-2 auto/heteroreceptors (boosting NE and 5-HT release) plus 5-HT2, 5-HT3, and H1 antagonism.
indications: Major depression, especially with insomnia, poor appetite, or weight loss; useful in cachectic/elderly patients.
side_effects: Sedation, increased appetite/weight gain, dry mouth; rare agranulocytosis.
pearl: Lower doses are MORE sedating (dominant antihistamine effect; offset by noradrenergic activity at higher doses). Appetite stimulation is a therapeutic perk.

name: Propranolol
moa: Competitively blocks beta-1 and beta-2; lowers HR, contractility, renin, and AV conduction; lipophilic/CNS-penetrant.
indications: HTN, angina, post-MI, arrhythmia, migraine prophylaxis, essential tremor, performance anxiety, thyroid storm, variceal bleed prophylaxis.
side_effects: Bronchospasm, bradycardia, fatigue, masks hypoglycemia, sexual dysfunction, vivid dreams.
pearl: Avoid in asthma (beta-2 bronchoconstriction). In thyroid storm also blocks peripheral T4->T3 conversion. Glucagon is the beta-blocker overdose antidote.

name: Timolol
moa: Blocks beta receptors on ciliary epithelium, decreasing aqueous humor production.
indications: Chronic open-angle glaucoma (lowers IOP); also oral for HTN/migraine.
side_effects: Systemic absorption can cause bradycardia and bronchospasm even from eye drops.
pearl: Lowers IOP by reducing aqueous production (vs prostaglandin analogs that increase outflow). Caution/contraindicated in asthma/COPD even as drops.

name: Nadolol
moa: Competitive non-selective beta-1/beta-2 antagonist; renally cleared, long half-life, minimal CNS penetration.
indications: HTN, angina, prophylaxis of esophageal variceal bleeding in cirrhosis, migraine.
side_effects: Bradycardia, fatigue, bronchospasm, accumulation in renal impairment.
pearl: Hydrophilic/once-daily - fewer CNS effects (less insomnia/nightmares) than propranolol. Used for variceal bleed prophylaxis.

name: Labetalol
moa: Blocks alpha-1 (vasodilation) plus beta-1/beta-2; beta effect predominates, lowering BP without strong reflex tachycardia.
indications: Hypertensive emergency, hypertension in pregnancy/preeclampsia.
side_effects: Orthostatic hypotension, dizziness, hepatotoxicity, bronchospasm, scalp tingling.
pearl: First-line for hypertension in pregnancy. Combined alpha+beta blockade avoids reflex tachycardia.

name: Carvedilol
moa: Blocks alpha-1, beta-1, beta-2; also antioxidant. Reduces afterload and provides cardioprotective beta-blockade.
indications: Chronic heart failure (HFrEF), hypertension, post-MI LV dysfunction.
side_effects: Dizziness, hypotension, bradycardia, fatigue, worsening HF if uptitrated too fast, bronchospasm.
pearl: One of three beta-blockers proven to reduce mortality in HFrEF (with metoprolol succinate and bisoprolol). Start low, go slow.

name: Sotalol
drug_class: Non-selective beta-blocker with Class III antiarrhythmic action
moa: Non-selective beta-blockade plus K+-channel blockade (Class III) that prolongs repolarization and the QT interval.
indications: Maintenance of sinus rhythm in atrial fibrillation/flutter; life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias.
side_effects: QT prolongation -> torsades de pointes, bradycardia, fatigue, bronchospasm.
pearl: Dual mechanism (beta-blocker + Class III). Requires inpatient initiation with QT/telemetry monitoring; renally cleared - dose by CrCl.

name: Acetazolamide
moa: Inhibits carbonic anhydrase in proximal tubule, blocking HCO3- reabsorption -> NaHCO3 diuresis and bicarbonaturia.
indications: Glaucoma, altitude sickness prophylaxis, metabolic alkalosis, idiopathic intracranial hypertension, urinary alkalinization.
side_effects: Hyperchloremic (normal anion gap) metabolic acidosis, paresthesias, hypokalemia, sulfa allergy, calcium phosphate stones.
pearl: Self-limited proximal RTA-like acidosis; alkaline urine promotes calcium phosphate stones. 'ACID'azolamide.

name: Mannitol
moa: Filtered, non-reabsorbed osmole raises tubular fluid osmolarity, drawing water into the lumen; acts mainly at proximal tubule/loop.
indications: Acute reduction of elevated ICP/intraocular pressure; maintaining urine flow/oliguria; some toxic ingestions.
side_effects: Pulmonary edema, early ECF expansion/hyponatremia, later dehydration/hypernatremia. Avoid in anuria, CHF, pulmonary edema.
pearl: Contraindicated in CHF/pulmonary edema and anuria: initial ECF volume expansion precedes diuresis.

name: Furosemide
moa: Inhibits Na-K-2Cl (NKCC2) in thick ascending limb; abolishes corticomedullary gradient. Stimulates PGE -> increased renal blood flow.
indications: Acute pulmonary edema, CHF, edematous states, hypertension, hypercalcemia, hyperkalemia.
side_effects: Ototoxicity, hypoKalemia, hypoMagnesemia, dehydration, sulfa Allergy, interstitial Nephritis, Gout/hyperuricemia; metabolic alkalosis, hypoCALCEMIA.
pearl: OH DANG mnemonic. Ototoxicity worsened by aminoglycosides. 'Loops lose calcium' (increase Ca2+ excretion) -> treat hypercalcemia.

name: Ethacrynic acid
moa: Inhibits Na-K-2Cl (NKCC2) cotransporter in thick ascending limb; same target as furosemide but a phenoxyacetic acid, not a sulfa drug.
indications: Diuresis/edema in patients with documented sulfa allergy who require a loop diuretic.
side_effects: Most ototoxic loop (often irreversible), hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, hyperuricemia, hypocalcemia, GI upset.
pearl: Loop of choice in sulfa-allergic patients; greatest/most irreversible ototoxicity among loops.

name: Bumetanide
moa: Inhibits Na-K-2Cl (NKCC2) cotransporter in thick ascending limb; ~40x more potent than furosemide by weight.
indications: CHF, edema, pulmonary edema; useful when high, reliable oral bioavailability is needed.
side_effects: Hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, ototoxicity (less than furosemide), hyperuricemia, hypovolemia, hypocalcemia.
pearl: High, predictable oral bioavailability (~80-100%); 1 mg bumetanide ~= 40 mg furosemide.

name: Torsemide
moa: Inhibits Na-K-2Cl (NKCC2) cotransporter in thick ascending limb; longer half-life and high, reliable oral bioavailability.
indications: Chronic CHF, edema, hypertension; favored for once-daily dosing.
side_effects: Hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, ototoxicity (lowest of loops), hyperuricemia, hypocalcemia.
pearl: Longest-acting loop; some data (TRANSFORM-HF was neutral) once suggested fewer HF readmissions vs furosemide.

name: Hydrochlorothiazide
moa: Inhibits Na-Cl (NCC) cotransporter in early distal convoluted tubule; enhances distal Ca2+ reabsorption -> hypocalciuria.
indications: Hypertension (first-line), mild CHF edema, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, idiopathic hypercalciuria/calcium stone prevention.
side_effects: hyperGLUC (Glycemia, Lipidemia, Uricemia, Calcemia). Hypokalemia, hyponatremia, metabolic alkalosis, sulfa allergy, pancreatitis.
pearl: 'Thiazides retain Calcium' (hypocalciuria but hyperCALCEMIA) -> prevent Ca2+ stones, opposite of loops.

name: Chlorthalidone
moa: Inhibits Na-Cl (NCC) cotransporter in distal convoluted tubule; much longer duration of action than HCTZ.
indications: Hypertension (preferred thiazide-type per many guidelines/trials), edema.
side_effects: More hypokalemia/hyponatremia than HCTZ; hyperglycemia, hyperuricemia, hypercalcemia, hyperlipidemia.
pearl: Longer half-life and strong CV outcome data (ALLHAT/SHEP); ~1.5-2x more potent than HCTZ for HTN.

name: Indapamide
moa: Inhibits distal tubule Na-Cl (NCC) cotransporter; also has direct vasodilatory properties.
indications: Hypertension, edema; used with perindopril in HYVET trial in very elderly.
side_effects: Hypokalemia, hyponatremia, hyperuricemia, QT prolongation; relatively lipid/glucose neutral at low dose.
pearl: Thiazide-like agent with added vasodilation; more metabolically neutral than HCTZ at low doses.

name: Metolazone
moa: Inhibits Na-Cl (NCC) cotransporter in DCT; retains efficacy at low GFR, unlike most thiazides.
indications: Adjunct to loop diuretics in diuretic-resistant CHF (sequential nephron blockade); edema.
side_effects: Profound hypokalemia and volume depletion when combined with a loop, hyponatremia, metabolic alkalosis.
pearl: Works even in renal insufficiency; classic add-on to a loop for resistant edema -> synergistic diuresis. Give ~30 min before loop.

name: Spironolactone
moa: Competitive mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist in cortical collecting duct; blocks aldosterone-driven Na+ reabsorption/K+ & H+ secretion.
indications: Hyperaldosteronism (Conn), HFrEF (RALES), cirrhosis/ascites, resistant HTN, PCOS/hirsutism/acne.
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, gynecomastia, decreased libido, antiandrogen effects, metabolic acidosis; rarely hyperkalemia-related arrhythmia.
pearl: Gynecomastia is the classic boards effect; mortality benefit in HFrEF. Watch hyperK with ACEi/ARB.

name: Eplerenone
moa: Selective mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist in collecting duct; spares androgen/progesterone receptors.
indications: HFrEF (EPHESUS, post-MI), resistant hypertension, hyperaldosteronism.
side_effects: Hyperkalemia (main); far fewer antiandrogen effects, minimal gynecomastia.
pearl: Choose over spironolactone when gynecomastia/sexual side effects are a concern; CYP3A4-metabolized.

name: Amiloride
moa: Directly blocks epithelial Na+ channel (ENaC) in collecting duct principal cells, reducing Na+ reabsorption and K+/H+ secretion.
indications: Lithium-induced nephrogenic DI, adjunct to thiazides/loops to limit K+ loss, Liddle syndrome.
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis; no antiandrogen/endocrine effects.
pearl: Drug of choice for lithium-induced nephrogenic DI (blocks Li+ entry via ENaC); treats Liddle syndrome.

name: Triamterene
moa: Blocks epithelial Na+ channel (ENaC) in collecting duct principal cells, independent of aldosterone.
indications: Combined with HCTZ (Dyazide) to offset K+ wasting; edema, hypertension.
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, nephrolithiasis (triamterene stones), can worsen renal function.
pearl: Can crystallize into triamterene kidney stones; commonly paired with HCTZ to prevent hypokalemia.

name: Captopril
moa: Inhibits ACE, blocking AngI to AngII conversion; lowers AngII and aldosterone and decreases bradykinin breakdown (vasodilation).
indications: Hypertension, heart failure (HFrEF), post-MI LV dysfunction, diabetic nephropathy/proteinuria.
side_effects: Dry cough, hyperkalemia, angioedema, acute renal failure (bilateral RAS), hypotension, rash/dysgeusia (sulfhydryl).
pearl: Prototype ACEi; sulfhydryl group uniquely causes rash and metallic taste. Short half-life means BID-TID dosing.

name: Enalapril
moa: Prodrug hydrolyzed to active enalaprilat; inhibits ACE, reducing AngII/aldosterone and decreasing bradykinin breakdown.
indications: Hypertension, HFrEF, asymptomatic LV dysfunction, diabetic nephropathy.
side_effects: Dry cough, hyperkalemia, angioedema, first-dose hypotension, azotemia; teratogenic (fetal renal damage).
pearl: Enalaprilat is the only IV ACE inhibitor (used for hypertensive emergencies).

name: Lisinopril
drug_class: ACE inhibitor (lysine analog, not a prodrug)
moa: Directly active (no hepatic activation); inhibits ACE to lower AngII and aldosterone and increase bradykinin.
indications: Hypertension, HFrEF, post-MI, diabetic and chronic kidney disease (renoprotection).
side_effects: Cough, hyperkalemia, angioedema, acute kidney injury, hypotension; contraindicated in pregnancy.
pearl: Most-prescribed ACEi; renally cleared with long half-life allowing once-daily dosing. No hepatic activation needed.

name: Ramipril
moa: Prodrug to ramiprilat; inhibits ACE, lowering AngII/aldosterone and raising bradykinin for vasodilation.
indications: Hypertension, HFrEF, post-MI; reduces CV events in high-risk patients (HOPE trial).
side_effects: Cough, hyperkalemia, angioedema, renal impairment, hypotension; fetal toxicity.
pearl: HOPE trial: lowers MI/stroke/death in high CV-risk patients even without HTN or low EF.

name: Losartan
drug_class: Angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB, AT1 antagonist)
moa: Selectively blocks AngII at AT1 receptor; prevents vasoconstriction and aldosterone release WITHOUT affecting bradykinin.
indications: Hypertension, HFrEF, diabetic nephropathy, ACEi-intolerant patients (cough).
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, acute kidney injury, hypotension; angioedema (rare); teratogenic in pregnancy.
pearl: Prototype ARB; minimal cough since bradykinin is spared. Uniquely uricosuric (inhibits URAT1), mildly lowering serum urate.

name: Valsartan
moa: Competitively blocks AT1 receptor, inhibiting AngII-mediated vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sodium retention.
indications: Hypertension, HFrEF, post-MI LV dysfunction; component of sacubitril/valsartan (ARNI).
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, renal dysfunction, hypotension, dizziness; angioedema (rare); contraindicated in pregnancy.
pearl: The ARB paired with sacubitril in Entresto (ARNI). Bradykinin-independent, so low cough risk.

name: Candesartan
moa: Prodrug (candesartan cilexetil) giving insurmountable AT1 blockade; tight, long receptor binding for sustained effect.
indications: Hypertension, HFrEF; also evidence base for migraine prophylaxis.
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, hypotension, renal impairment, dizziness; teratogenic.
pearl: Strong HFrEF data (CHARM trials); insurmountable/long-acting receptor binding gives durable BP control.

name: Aliskiren
moa: Directly binds and inhibits renin, blocking angiotensinogen to AngI conversion — blocks RAAS at its first/rate-limiting step.
indications: Hypertension (typically second-line/adjunct).
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, hypotension, diarrhea (dose-dependent), angioedema, renal dysfunction.
pearl: Do NOT combine with ACEi/ARB in diabetics (ALTITUDE: increased renal failure, stroke, hyperkalemia). Contraindicated in pregnancy.

name: Spironolactone
moa: Competitively antagonizes mineralocorticoid receptor in cortical collecting duct; blocks aldosterone, promoting Na+/water loss and K+ retention.
indications: HFrEF (RALES), primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn), resistant HTN, ascites/cirrhosis, hirsutism/PCOS.
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, gynecomastia, antiandrogen effects (impotence, menstrual irregularity), hyperkalemic (type 4 RTA) metabolic acidosis.
pearl: Nonselective steroid-receptor binding causes gynecomastia; RALES showed mortality benefit in severe HFrEF.

name: Eplerenone
moa: Selective mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist; blocks aldosterone without significant androgen/progesterone receptor activity.
indications: HFrEF post-MI (EPHESUS), hypertension, heart failure.
side_effects: Hyperkalemia (key risk); minimal endocrine effects (little gynecomastia).
pearl: Selective MR antagonist — avoids gynecomastia, so preferred over spironolactone when sexual side effects matter.

name: Sacubitril/valsartan
moa: Sacubitril (via sacubitrilat) inhibits neprilysin, raising natriuretic peptides/bradykinin; valsartan blocks AT1 — vasodilation/natriuresis.
indications: HFrEF (superior to enalapril in PARADIGM-HF); reduces HF hospitalization and CV death.
side_effects: Hypotension, hyperkalemia, renal impairment, angioedema (higher neprilysin-related risk); cough less than ACEi.
pearl: Requires 36 h washout when switching from an ACEi (overlapping bradykinin -> angioedema). Never combine with ACEi.

name: Benazepril
moa: Prodrug to benazeprilat; inhibits ACE to reduce AngII/aldosterone and slow bradykinin degradation.
indications: Hypertension; combined with amlodipine in fixed-dose combos for HTN.
side_effects: Cough, hyperkalemia, angioedema, renal dysfunction, hypotension; teratogenic.
pearl: ACCOMPLISH trial: benazepril + amlodipine beat benazepril + HCTZ for CV outcomes in high-risk HTN.

name: Irbesartan
moa: Selective AT1 antagonist; blocks AngII vasoconstriction and aldosterone release; bradykinin-independent.
indications: Hypertension, type 2 diabetic nephropathy (renoprotection; IDNT and IRMA-2 trials).
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, hypotension, renal impairment, dizziness; contraindicated in pregnancy.
pearl: IDNT showed slowed progression of diabetic nephropathy independent of BP lowering.

name: Telmisartan
drug_class: Angiotensin II receptor blocker (ARB, partial PPAR-gamma agonist)
moa: AT1 receptor blockade plus partial PPAR-gamma agonism, conferring favorable metabolic (insulin-sensitizing) effects.
indications: Hypertension; CV risk reduction in ACEi-intolerant patients (ONTARGET/TRANSCEND).
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, hypotension, renal dysfunction; teratogenic; back pain/sinusitis.
pearl: Longest ARB half-life (~24 h) and PPAR-gamma activity. ONTARGET: ARB+ACEi combo adds renal harm without benefit.

name: Propranolol
drug_class: Nonselective beta blocker (β1 = β2), 1st generation, lipophilic
moa: Competitive antagonist at β1 and β2 receptors; ↓ cAMP → ↓ HR, contractility, AV conduction, and renin release; lipophilic, crosses BBB.
indications: HTN, angina, post-MI, arrhythmias, migraine prophylaxis, essential tremor, performance anxiety, hyperthyroidism/thyroid storm.
side_effects: Bradycardia, AV block, fatigue, bronchospasm, masks hypoglycemia (except sweating), vivid dreams/CNS effects, sexual dysfunction.
pearl: Prototype nonselective BB. High lipophilicity = CNS penetration (nightmares). Used in thyroid storm; high doses inhibit 5'-deiodinase (↓ T4→T3).

name: Metoprolol
moa: Selective β1 antagonism → ↓ HR, contractility, AV nodal conduction; spares β2 (bronchial/vascular) tone at usual doses.
indications: HTN, angina, post-MI (cardioprotection), HFrEF (succinate form), SVT/rate control in AFib.
side_effects: Bradycardia, AV block, fatigue, hypotension; safer in COPD/asthma than nonselective but still use caution.
pearl: Metoprolol succinate (XL) is the HF-approved form; tartrate is short-acting (BID). β1-selectivity is dose-dependent (lost at high doses).

name: Atenolol
moa: Selective β1 antagonist; hydrophilic → minimal CNS penetration; renally eliminated.
indications: HTN, angina, post-MI, arrhythmias; historically common but less preferred for primary HTN now.
side_effects: Bradycardia, fatigue, cold extremities; accumulates in renal failure (dose adjust).
pearl: Hydrophilic → fewer CNS side effects than propranolol; renally cleared (vs hepatic metoprolol). Inferior stroke/outcome data in some HTN trials.

name: Esmolol
moa: Selective β1 antagonist; rapidly hydrolyzed by RBC esterases (t½ ~9 min) → highly titratable, quickly reversible.
indications: Acute SVT, perioperative/intraoperative HTN & tachycardia, aortic dissection rate control, hypertensive emergency.
side_effects: Hypotension, bradycardia; resolves quickly after stopping infusion.
pearl: Shortest-acting BB; metabolized by plasma/RBC esterases (not liver/kidney). Ideal for rapid on/off control in unstable patients.

name: Carvedilol
moa: Blocks β1, β2, and α1 receptors → cardiac β-blockade plus α1-mediated vasodilation.
indications: HFrEF (mortality benefit), HTN, post-MI LV dysfunction.
side_effects: Hypotension/orthostasis (α1), dizziness, bradycardia, fatigue; can worsen acute decompensated HF if started during decompensation.
pearl: One of 3 BBs proven to ↓ mortality in HFrEF (with metoprolol succinate & bisoprolol). α1-block adds BP lowering.

name: Labetalol
moa: Combined α- and β-blockade; β:α ratio ~7:1 IV and ~3:1 oral → vasodilation plus cardiac slowing.
indications: Hypertensive emergency, HTN in pregnancy/preeclampsia.
side_effects: Orthostatic hypotension, scalp tingling, dizziness, hepatotoxicity (rare), bradycardia.
pearl: First-line IV agent for hypertensive emergency and chronic HTN in pregnancy. Avoid as monotherapy in pheochromocytoma (risk of unopposed α).

name: Bisoprolol
moa: Highly β1-selective antagonist → ↓ HR and contractility with minimal β2 effects.
indications: HFrEF (mortality benefit), HTN, angina, rate control.
side_effects: Bradycardia, fatigue, cold extremities, hypotension.
pearl: Third of the trio of mortality-reducing HF beta blockers (with carvedilol & metoprolol succinate). Among the most β1-selective.

name: Nebivolol
drug_class: Cardioselective beta blocker (β1-selective) with NO-mediated vasodilation, 3rd generation
moa: Highly β1-selective antagonist that also stimulates endothelial nitric oxide (NO) release → vasodilation and ↓ SVR.
indications: HTN (favorable metabolic/vascular profile).
side_effects: Headache, fatigue, dizziness; generally well tolerated, fewer metabolic effects.
pearl: Unique mechanism: β1-block PLUS NO-dependent vasodilation. Most β1-selective oral BB; metabolically neutral.

name: Pindolol
drug_class: Nonselective beta blocker with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity (ISA / partial agonist)
moa: Partial β-agonist: antagonizes at high catecholamine states but provides mild agonism at rest → less resting bradycardia.
indications: HTN (esp. patients prone to bradycardia); largely historical.
side_effects: Less bradycardia and fewer adverse lipid effects than other BBs; otherwise typical BB effects.
pearl: Classic ISA prototype. Avoid post-MI and in HF/angina—ISA may worsen outcomes. Less bradycardia and less lipid derangement.

name: Acebutolol
drug_class: Cardioselective beta blocker (β1-selective) with intrinsic sympathomimetic activity (ISA)
moa: β1-selective partial agonist; mild intrinsic agonism limits resting bradycardia.
indications: HTN, ventricular arrhythmias (PVCs).
side_effects: Less bradycardia (ISA); can cause drug-induced lupus (positive ANA).
pearl: β1-selective BB WITH ISA. Associated with drug-induced lupus. ISA agents are avoided post-MI.

name: Timolol
moa: Nonselective β-block; in the eye ↓ aqueous humor PRODUCTION by ciliary epithelium → ↓ intraocular pressure.
indications: Open-angle glaucoma (topical), HTN, migraine prophylaxis (oral).
side_effects: Topical can be systemically absorbed → bradycardia, bronchospasm; avoid in asthma/COPD.
pearl: Classic topical glaucoma BB—decreases aqueous humor PRODUCTION. Eye drops can cause systemic β-blockade (bronchospasm in asthmatics).

name: Sotalol
drug_class: Nonselective beta blocker + Class III antiarrhythmic (K+ channel blocker)
moa: β-blockade (Class II) plus K+ channel blockade (Class III) → prolongs action potential/refractory period and QT.
indications: Maintenance of sinus rhythm in AFib/flutter; life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias.
side_effects: QT prolongation → torsades de pointes, bradycardia; requires inpatient initiation with monitoring.
pearl: Dual Class II + III action. QT prolongation = torsades risk; renally cleared, started with telemetry. A rhythm-control antiarrhythmic, not a typical BB.

name: Nadolol
moa: Nonselective β1/β2 antagonist; hydrophilic, renally cleared, very long half-life → once-daily dosing.
indications: HTN, angina, prophylaxis of variceal bleeding in portal hypertension, migraine.
side_effects: Bradycardia, fatigue, bronchospasm (nonselective); accumulates in renal impairment.
pearl: Nonselective BB used for variceal bleeding prophylaxis (↓ portal pressure). Long half-life, renally eliminated, minimal CNS penetration.

name: Glucagon
drug_class: Beta-blocker overdose antidote (not a beta blocker)
moa: Activates cardiac adenylyl cyclase via its own GPCR—bypassing the blocked β-receptor—→ ↑ cAMP → ↑ HR and contractility.
indications: First-line antidote for beta-blocker (and calcium channel blocker) overdose with bradycardia/hypotension.
side_effects: Nausea/vomiting, hyperglycemia, hypokalemia.
pearl: Classic board answer: glucagon reverses BB overdose by raising cAMP independent of β-receptors. Pair with atropine/IV fluids/pacing.

name: Amlodipine
moa: Blocks L-type Ca2+ channels preferentially in vascular smooth muscle, causing arterial vasodilation and reduced peripheral resistance.
indications: Hypertension, chronic stable & vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina.
side_effects: Peripheral edema (dose-dependent), flushing, headache, dizziness; reflex tachycardia (less than short-acting DHPs).
pearl: Longest half-life of CCBs (smooth, once-daily). Edema is from precapillary arteriolar dilation, NOT fluid overload, so diuretics don't help; add ACEi/ARB.

name: Nifedipine
moa: Potent L-type Ca2+ channel blockade of vascular smooth muscle; minimal cardiac depression at usual doses.
indications: Hypertension, angina, Raynaud phenomenon; tocolysis in preterm labor.
side_effects: Reflex tachycardia, flushing, headache, peripheral edema; immediate-release form linked to increased mortality.
pearl: Never use immediate-release nifedipine for hypertensive emergency: reflex tachycardia can precipitate MI/stroke. Use extended-release.

name: Nimodipine
moa: DHP with high CNS lipophilicity; blocks L-type Ca2+ channels in cerebral vasculature.
indications: Prevention of cerebral vasospasm/delayed ischemia after subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH).
side_effects: Hypotension, headache, flushing, edema.
pearl: The CCB for SAH: reduces delayed ischemic deficits. Give ORALLY only; IV use caused fatal cardiovascular collapse (boxed warning).

name: Clevidipine
moa: Rapidly metabolized by blood/tissue esterases; selective arterial vasodilation via L-type Ca2+ blockade.
indications: Hypertensive emergency/perioperative BP control (IV infusion).
side_effects: Hypotension, reflex tachycardia, headache, nausea; lipid load (it's a lipid emulsion).
pearl: Lipid emulsion (like propofol) with ~1 min half-life; esterase metabolism makes titration independent of renal/hepatic function. Contraindicated in soy/egg allergy, defective lipid metabolism, severe aortic stenosis.

name: Verapamil
moa: Blocks L-type Ca2+ channels in cardiac myocytes & nodal tissue: negative inotrope/chronotrope/dromotrope, slows AV conduction.
indications: HTN, angina, SVT/AFib rate control, cluster headache prophylaxis.
side_effects: Constipation (most common), bradycardia, AV block, heart failure exacerbation, gingival hyperplasia.
pearl: Most cardiodepressant/negative-inotropic CCB (least vascular selectivity). Avoid with beta-blockers (additive AV block) and in HFrEF. Inhibits CYP3A4/P-gp (raises digoxin).

name: Diltiazem
moa: Intermediate vascular/cardiac selectivity; blocks L-type Ca2+ channels to slow AV node and cause vasodilation.
indications: HTN, angina (incl. vasospastic), AFib/atrial flutter & SVT rate control.
side_effects: Bradycardia, AV block, edema, constipation (less than verapamil), HF exacerbation.
pearl: Like verapamil, avoid combining with beta-blockers. IV diltiazem is a go-to for acute AFib rate control with preserved EF.

name: Hydralazine
moa: Increases cGMP causing selective arteriolar (not venous) smooth muscle relaxation, reducing afterload.
indications: HTN (esp. pregnancy), HFrEF with isosorbide dinitrate (esp. Black patients).
side_effects: Reflex tachycardia, fluid retention, headache; drug-induced lupus (SLE-like) syndrome.
pearl: Classic cause of drug-induced lupus (with procainamide/isoniazid), more in slow acetylators. Co-administer beta-blocker + diuretic to offset reflexes.

name: Sodium nitroprusside
moa: Releases nitric oxide, increasing cGMP to dilate both arteries and veins (reduces preload AND afterload).
indications: Hypertensive emergency, acute decompensated HF, controlled intraoperative hypotension.
side_effects: Hypotension, reflex tachycardia; CYANIDE & thiocyanate toxicity (metabolic acidosis, AMS) esp. with renal/hepatic impairment.
pearl: Cyanide toxicity from prolonged/high-dose infusion. Antidotes: sodium thiosulfate, hydroxocobalamin, sodium nitrite. Light-sensitive.

name: Fenoldopam
moa: Stimulates peripheral D1 receptors causing arteriolar vasodilation and increased renal blood flow (natriuresis/diuresis).
indications: Hypertensive emergency, especially with renal insufficiency.
side_effects: Reflex tachycardia, hypotension, headache, flushing; raises intraocular pressure (caution in glaucoma).
pearl: The HTN-emergency drug that PROTECTS the kidney by promoting renal perfusion and natriuresis. Use caution in glaucoma (raises IOP).

name: Clonidine
moa: Stimulates central presynaptic alpha-2 receptors, decreasing sympathetic outflow and lowering BP/HR.
indications: Resistant HTN, ADHD, opioid/alcohol withdrawal, hot flashes, Tourette syndrome.
side_effects: Sedation, dry mouth, bradycardia; severe REBOUND hypertension if stopped abruptly.
pearl: Never stop abruptly: rebound hypertensive crisis. If on a beta-blocker, taper/stop the beta-blocker first to avoid unopposed alpha effect. Safe in renal disease.

name: Methyldopa
moa: Converted to alpha-methylnorepinephrine, a central alpha-2 agonist that decreases sympathetic outflow.
indications: Hypertension in pregnancy (classic).
side_effects: Sedation, positive Coombs test/hemolytic anemia, drug-induced hepatitis, lupus-like syndrome, edema.
pearl: Go-to antihypertensive in pregnancy. Causes a positive direct Coombs test (usually without overt hemolysis) and can elevate LFTs.

name: Prazosin
moa: Blocks postsynaptic alpha-1 receptors, relaxing arterial/venous smooth muscle and bladder neck/prostate.
indications: HTN (rarely first-line), BPH, PTSD-associated nightmares.
side_effects: First-dose orthostatic hypotension/syncope, dizziness, reflex tachycardia, nasal congestion.
pearl: Classic FIRST-DOSE phenomenon: severe orthostatic hypotension; give first dose at bedtime. Doxazosin/terazosin are longer-acting analogs.

name: Aliskiren
moa: Directly inhibits renin, blocking conversion of angiotensinogen to angiotensin I (suppresses RAAS upstream).
indications: Hypertension (monotherapy or adjunct).
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, angioedema, cough (less than ACEi), diarrhea at high doses; teratogenic.
pearl: Do NOT combine with ACEi/ARB (esp. in diabetes/renal impairment): increased renal failure, hyperkalemia, hypotension. Contraindicated in pregnancy.

name: Minoxidil
moa: Opens ATP-sensitive K+ channels, hyperpolarizing and relaxing arteriolar smooth muscle to reduce afterload.
indications: Severe/refractory hypertension; topical form for androgenetic alopecia.
side_effects: Marked reflex tachycardia, salt/water retention, pericardial effusion; HYPERTRICHOSIS.
pearl: Causes hypertrichosis (basis of topical use for hair loss). Co-administer a beta-blocker AND loop diuretic to counter reflex tachycardia and fluid retention.

name: Quinidine
drug_class: Class IA (Na+ channel blocker, intermediate kinetics)
moa: Blocks open fast Na+ channels (slows phase 0, widens QRS) and K+ channels (prolongs phase 3, lengthens AP/QT); also alpha-blocking and antimuscarinic (vagolytic) effects.
indications: Atrial fibrillation/flutter, SVT, ventricular arrhythmias; rarely used now. Drug of choice in Brugada and short-QT syndromes.
side_effects: Cinchonism (tinnitus, headache, vertigo), torsades de pointes, thrombocytopenia, hemolysis (G6PD), diarrhea/GI, hypotension.
pearl: Cinchonism is the classic clue. Inhibits P-gp -> raises digoxin levels. Give AV nodal blocker first in AFib (vagolytic effect risks 1:1 conduction).

name: Procainamide
moa: Blocks fast Na+ channels (slows phase 0) and K+ channels (prolongs AP/QT); hepatic acetylation yields NAPA, which has pure Class III activity.
indications: Stable monomorphic VT, AFib; preferred for WPW with AFib (pre-excited AF); SVT.
side_effects: Drug-induced lupus (anti-histone Abs), agranulocytosis, torsades/QT prolongation, hypotension.
pearl: Classic cause of drug-induced SLE (slow acetylators at higher risk). Preferred in WPW + AFib. NAPA (renally cleared) accumulates in renal failure -> torsades.

name: Disopyramide
moa: Blocks fast Na+ and K+ channels (slows phase 0, prolongs AP/QT); strongest negative inotrope of IA agents; potent antimuscarinic.
indications: Ventricular arrhythmias; hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (negative inotropy reduces LVOT obstruction); vagally-mediated AFib.
side_effects: Marked anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision), heart failure (negative inotropy), torsades.
pearl: Most negative inotropy of Class IA, leveraged in HOCM. Avoid in HFrEF and glaucoma/BPH. Strong anticholinergic profile distinguishes it.

name: Lidocaine
drug_class: Class IB (Na+ channel blocker, fast kinetics)
moa: Blocks fast Na+ channels with rapid on/off; preferentially blocks depolarized/ischemic (open or inactivated) tissue; shortens AP duration in Purkinje/ventricle.
indications: Ventricular arrhythmias, especially post-MI/ischemia and digoxin-induced; not effective for atrial arrhythmias.
side_effects: CNS toxicity (perioral numbness, tremor, slurred speech, seizures, confusion), cardiovascular depression at high doses.
pearl: IB drugs are best in ischemic/post-MI ventricular tissue. CNS effects are dose-limiting. Given IV/IM only (extensive hepatic first-pass metabolism).

name: Mexiletine
moa: Oral lidocaine analog; blocks fast Na+ channels (rapid kinetics), shortens AP duration; preferentially affects depolarized ischemic tissue.
indications: Chronic ventricular arrhythmias; also used for neuropathic pain and myotonia (LQT3, myotonic dystrophy).
side_effects: Tremor, dizziness, nausea/GI upset, ataxia; CNS effects similar to lidocaine.
pearl: The orally active 'lidocaine for chronic use.' Useful in LQT3 (Na+ channel gain-of-function, late current) and myotonia.

name: Flecainide
drug_class: Class IC (Na+ channel blocker, slow kinetics)
moa: Markedly slows phase 0 (strongest Na+ block, slow dissociation) with minimal effect on AP duration; pronounced QRS widening, use-dependent.
indications: SVT and AFib in patients WITHOUT structural heart disease ('pill-in-pocket'); AVNRT/AVRT.
side_effects: Proarrhythmic (incessant VT), worsens conduction; contraindicated in structural/ischemic heart disease.
pearl: CAST trial: increased mortality post-MI. Avoid in structural heart disease. Can organize AFib to slow flutter with 1:1 conduction; pair with AV nodal blocker.

name: Propafenone
moa: Strong fast Na+ channel block, slow kinetics, marked QRS widening; also has weak beta-blocking activity.
indications: Paroxysmal AFib/SVT in patients without structural heart disease; 'pill-in-pocket.'
side_effects: Metallic taste, proarrhythmia, bronchospasm (beta-blockade), GI upset, dizziness.
pearl: Like flecainide, contraindicated in structural/ischemic heart disease (CAST lesson). Mild beta-blockade distinguishes it; metallic taste is a clue.

name: Metoprolol
moa: Blocks beta-1 receptors, decreasing cAMP and Ca2+ currents; slows SA node automaticity and AV nodal conduction (slows phase 4, prolongs PR).
indications: Rate control in AFib/flutter, SVT, post-MI (mortality benefit), exercise/catecholamine-induced arrhythmias.
side_effects: Bradycardia, AV block, hypotension, fatigue, bronchospasm (less than nonselective), masks hypoglycemia symptoms.
pearl: Class II works mainly at the AV node. Beta-blockers are first-line for rate control and have proven mortality benefit post-MI.

name: Esmolol
moa: Beta-1 selective antagonist with very short half-life (~9 min, RBC esterase metabolism); slows AV conduction and SA automaticity.
indications: Acute SVT, intraoperative/perioperative tachycardia and HTN, rapid rate control when titratability needed.
side_effects: Hypotension, bradycardia, AV block; effects resolve quickly on stopping infusion.
pearl: Ultra-short half-life via RBC esterases (not plasma cholinesterase) makes it ideal when you want a quickly reversible beta-blocker (unstable/perioperative).

name: Amiodarone
drug_class: Class III (K+ channel blocker; has I/II/III/IV activity)
moa: Blocks K+ channels (prolongs AP/QT) plus Na+, beta, and Ca2+ channel blockade; greatly prolongs refractory period and slows conduction.
indications: Most arrhythmias: VT/VF, AFib/flutter, SVT; first-line in many ventricular arrhythmias.
side_effects: Pulmonary fibrosis, hepatotoxicity, thyroid dysfunction (hypo/hyper), blue-gray skin, corneal microdeposits, photosensitivity, neuropathy.
pearl: Check PFTs, LFTs, TFTs before/during use. Iodine-containing -> thyroid effects. Despite QT prolongation, torsades is rare. Long half-life (weeks-months).

name: Sotalol
drug_class: Class III (K+ channel blocker) + Class II (nonselective beta-blocker)
moa: Nonselective beta-blockade (Class II) plus K+ channel block prolonging AP/QT (Class III); slows AV conduction and prolongs refractoriness.
indications: Ventricular arrhythmias, maintenance of sinus rhythm in AFib.
side_effects: Torsades de pointes (dose-dependent QT prolongation), bradycardia, fatigue, bronchospasm.
pearl: High torsades risk; initiate inpatient with QT/renal monitoring (renally cleared). Has both Class II and III activity.

name: Dofetilide
drug_class: Class III (pure IKr K+ channel blocker)
moa: Selectively blocks the rapid delayed rectifier K+ current (IKr), prolonging AP duration/refractory period and QT.
indications: Conversion and maintenance of sinus rhythm in AFib/flutter.
side_effects: Torsades de pointes (significant QT prolongation), headache; renally cleared.
pearl: Must be initiated in hospital with continuous ECG/QT monitoring for ~3 days; dose adjusted for renal function/QT. Pure IKr blocker.

name: Verapamil
moa: Blocks L-type Ca2+ channels in SA/AV nodes; slows phase 0/4 of nodal action potentials, prolonging AV conduction (PR) and refractoriness.
indications: Rate control in AFib/flutter, SVT (AV-nodal reentrant); idiopathic/fascicular VT.
side_effects: Constipation, bradycardia, AV block, hypotension, peripheral edema, negative inotropy (avoid in HFrEF), gingival hyperplasia.
pearl: Avoid in WPW with AFib (can accelerate accessory pathway -> VF) and in HFrEF. Inhibits P-gp -> raises digoxin levels. Constipation is classic.

name: Adenosine
moa: Activates A1 receptors -> opens K+ channels (hyperpolarizes) and decreases cAMP/Ca2+; transiently blocks AV nodal conduction.
indications: First-line for acute termination of paroxysmal SVT (AVNRT/AVRT); diagnostic unmasking in atrial flutter/wide-complex tachycardia.
side_effects: Transient flushing, chest pain/pressure, dyspnea/bronchospasm, brief sinus pause/AV block, sense of impending doom.
pearl: Very short half-life (<10 sec); give rapid IV push with saline flush. Blocked by caffeine/theophylline (adenosine antagonists); potentiated by dipyridamole.

name: Digoxin
moa: Inhibits Na+/K+-ATPase -> raises intracellular Ca2+ (positive inotropy); increases vagal tone -> slows AV conduction and SA automaticity.
indications: Rate control in AFib (esp. with HFrEF or hypotension), heart failure symptom control.
side_effects: Narrow therapeutic index: nausea/vomiting, visual changes (yellow-green halos), confusion, arrhythmias; hyperkalemia in acute toxicity.
pearl: Antidote is digoxin-specific antibody fragments (Fab). Chronic toxicity worsened by HYPOkalemia, hypomagnesemia, renal failure. Yellow vision is classic.

name: Nitroglycerin
moa: Releases NO -> activates guanylate cyclase -> increased cGMP -> smooth muscle relaxation; predominantly venodilation reducing preload and wall tension/O2 demand.
indications: Acute angina (sublingual), unstable angina/ACS, acute decompensated HF/pulmonary edema, hypertensive emergency.
side_effects: Throbbing headache, reflex tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension, flushing; tolerance with continuous use.
pearl: Contraindicated with PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil) -> severe hypotension. Use nitrate-free interval to avoid tolerance.

name: Isosorbide dinitrate
moa: NO donor -> increased cGMP -> venodilation (preload reduction); metabolized to active isosorbide mononitrate, prolonging effect.
indications: Chronic stable angina prophylaxis; HFrEF (with hydralazine, esp. in self-identified Black patients).
side_effects: Headache, hypotension, reflex tachycardia, tolerance.
pearl: Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate (BiDil) improves HFrEF survival, esp. in Black patients; hydralazine blunts nitrate tolerance.

name: Isosorbide mononitrate
moa: NO donor -> increased cGMP -> venodilation reducing preload; near-complete bioavailability (no first-pass metabolism).
indications: Chronic stable angina prophylaxis (once-daily dosing).
side_effects: Headache, hypotension, reflex tachycardia, tolerance.
pearl: Active metabolite of isosorbide dinitrate; eccentric (asymmetric) dosing provides a nitrate-free interval to prevent tolerance.

name: Digoxin
moa: Inhibits Na+/K+-ATPase -> increased intracellular Na+ -> less Ca2+ extrusion via NCX -> increased intracellular Ca2+ -> positive inotropy; increases vagal tone, slowing AV conduction.
indications: HFrEF symptom control (reduces hospitalizations, no mortality benefit); rate control in atrial fibrillation.
side_effects: GI upset, blurred/yellow-green vision (xanthopsia), confusion; arrhythmias (AV block, accelerated junctional, bidirectional VT).
pearl: Toxicity worsened by hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and renal failure (renally cleared). Antidote = digoxin-specific Fab fragments (DigiFab). Narrow therapeutic index.

name: Ranolazine
moa: Inhibits the late inward Na+ current -> reduces Na+-dependent Ca2+ overload -> decreases ventricular wall tension and O2 demand; no significant effect on HR or BP.
indications: Chronic stable angina (typically add-on when nitrates/beta-blockers/CCBs are inadequate).
side_effects: Constipation, dizziness, nausea, headache; QT prolongation.
pearl: Antianginal that does NOT lower HR or BP; CYP3A4 substrate. Prolongs QT but has notably low torsades risk.

name: Ivabradine
moa: Selectively blocks HCN ('funny') channels in the SA node -> slows diastolic depolarization -> lowers heart rate with no effect on contractility or BP.
indications: Chronic stable angina; HFrEF in sinus rhythm with HR >=70 on max-tolerated beta-blocker (reduces hospitalizations).
side_effects: Bradycardia, atrial fibrillation; luminous phosphenes (transient visual brightness/flashes).
pearl: Classic association: phosphenes from retinal HCN channel block. Pure HR-lowering, no inotropy. Avoid with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors.

name: Metoprolol succinate
moa: Blocks cardiac beta-1 receptors -> decreased HR, contractility, and AV conduction -> reduced myocardial O2 demand; long-term reverse remodeling benefit in HF.
indications: Chronic stable angina, HFrEF (guideline beta-blocker), post-MI, hypertension, rate control.
side_effects: Bradycardia, fatigue, hypotension, masking of hypoglycemia; bronchospasm at high doses; avoid abrupt withdrawal.
pearl: One of three beta-blockers proven to reduce HFrEF mortality (with carvedilol, bisoprolol). Start low, titrate slowly; never start during acute decompensation.

name: Carvedilol
moa: Blocks beta-1, beta-2, and alpha-1 receptors -> reduced HR/contractility plus vasodilation (afterload reduction); also has antioxidant properties.
indications: HFrEF (mortality benefit), post-MI LV dysfunction, hypertension, angina.
side_effects: Bradycardia, hypotension/dizziness, fatigue; bronchospasm (nonselective); can mask hypoglycemia.
pearl: Preferred HF beta-blocker; alpha-1 blockade adds afterload reduction. Mortality-proven in HFrEF.

name: Amlodipine
moa: Blocks L-type Ca2+ channels preferentially in vascular smooth muscle -> arterial vasodilation -> reduced afterload and coronary vasodilation; minimal direct cardiac effect.
indications: Chronic stable & vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina, hypertension.
side_effects: Peripheral edema, flushing, headache; reflex tachycardia (minimal due to long half-life).
pearl: Safe in HFrEF (unlike non-DHP CCBs) - no negative inotropy. Edema is from precapillary dilation, not fluid overload (doesn't respond to diuretics).

name: Diltiazem
moa: Blocks L-type Ca2+ channels in heart and vessels -> slows SA/AV nodal conduction, decreases HR/contractility, mild vasodilation -> reduced O2 demand.
indications: Chronic stable & vasospastic angina, rate control in AF/SVT, hypertension.
side_effects: Bradycardia, AV block, hypotension, constipation; edema.
pearl: Avoid in HFrEF and combined with beta-blockers (additive AV block/negative inotropy). Non-DHP CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) are rate-controlling.

name: Verapamil
moa: Greatest myocardial depressant effect of the CCBs; strongly blocks L-type Ca2+ channels at the SA/AV node -> marked negative chronotropy/dromotropy/inotropy.
indications: Angina, SVT/AF rate control, hypertension.
side_effects: Constipation (classic), bradycardia, AV block, hypotension; worsens HF.
pearl: Constipation is the hallmark side effect. Contraindicated in HFrEF and with beta-blockers. Raises serum digoxin levels.

name: Sacubitril/valsartan
moa: Sacubitril (neprilysin inhibitor) raises natriuretic peptides -> vasodilation/natriuresis; valsartan blocks the AT1 receptor -> blunts RAAS/aldosterone.
indications: HFrEF (replaces ACEi/ARB; reduces mortality and hospitalization); also used in HFmrEF/HFpEF.
side_effects: Hypotension, hyperkalemia, renal impairment, angioedema.
pearl: Allow 36-hour washout from an ACE inhibitor before starting (additive angioedema risk). Superior to enalapril (PARADIGM-HF).

name: Dapagliflozin
moa: Blocks renal SGLT2 -> glucosuria/natriuresis -> osmotic diuresis, preload reduction, and cardioprotective metabolic/hemodynamic effects (mechanism not fully defined).
indications: HFrEF and HFpEF (reduces HF hospitalization/CV death regardless of diabetes status), T2DM, CKD.
side_effects: Genital/urinary infections, volume depletion/hypotension, euglycemic DKA, Fournier gangrene (rare).
pearl: A pillar of guideline-directed HF therapy ('four pillars': ARNI/ACEi, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i) - benefits even non-diabetics.

name: Atorvastatin
moa: Competitively inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme of hepatic cholesterol synthesis; depletes intracellular cholesterol, upregulating LDL receptors and increasing LDL clearance.
indications: First-line for hypercholesterolemia; ASCVD primary and secondary prevention.
side_effects: Myalgia/myopathy, rare rhabdomyolysis, hepatotoxicity (transaminitis), new-onset diabetes, GI upset.
pearl: High-intensity statin with long half-life (any-time dosing). CYP3A4 substrate; myopathy risk rises with gemfibrozil and CYP3A4 inhibitors.

name: Rosuvastatin
moa: Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase; high-potency hydrophilic statin with a long half-life, producing strong LDL lowering and modest HDL rise.
indications: High-intensity LDL lowering; ASCVD prevention; useful when most potent effect needed.
side_effects: Myalgia/myopathy, rare rhabdomyolysis, hepatotoxicity, proteinuria/hematuria (dose-related), new-onset diabetes.
pearl: Most potent statin per mg; hydrophilic and minimally CYP-metabolized, so fewer drug interactions than atorvastatin/simvastatin.

name: Simvastatin
moa: Lipophilic prodrug that inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, lowering hepatic cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors.
indications: Hypercholesterolemia; ASCVD risk reduction (dose in the evening).
side_effects: Myalgia/myopathy, rhabdomyolysis (highest risk at 80 mg), hepatotoxicity, new-onset diabetes.
pearl: CYP3A4 substrate; grapefruit juice, amiodarone, and gemfibrozil markedly raise rhabdomyolysis risk. The 80 mg dose is restricted.

name: Ezetimibe
moa: Blocks the NPC1L1 sterol transporter on intestinal brush-border enterocytes, reducing dietary/biliary cholesterol absorption and increasing hepatic LDL receptor expression.
indications: Add-on to statin when LDL goal unmet; statin-intolerant patients; sitosterolemia.
side_effects: Generally well tolerated; rare diarrhea, myalgia, and transaminase elevation (more when combined with a statin).
pearl: Acts at NPC1L1 in the gut, not the liver. IMPROVE-IT: statin + ezetimibe lowered events more than statin alone.

name: Cholestyramine
moa: Binds bile acids in the gut, preventing reabsorption; liver diverts cholesterol to make new bile acids, upregulating LDL receptors and lowering LDL.
indications: LDL lowering (add-on); pruritus of cholestasis (bile acid accumulation); bile acid (e.g., post-cholecystectomy) diarrhea.
side_effects: GI: constipation, bloating, nausea; can raise triglycerides; malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamins (ADEK).
pearl: Not absorbed systemically (safe in pregnancy). Binds other drugs (warfarin, digoxin, levothyroxine, thiazides)—separate dosing by several hours.

name: Fenofibrate
moa: Activates PPAR-alpha, increasing lipoprotein lipase activity and apo A-I/A-II while decreasing apo C-III; markedly lowers triglycerides and modestly raises HDL.
indications: Severe hypertriglyceridemia (prevent pancreatitis); mixed dyslipidemia; first-line for very high TG.
side_effects: Myopathy (esp. with statins), cholelithiasis (gallstones), hepatotoxicity, reversible creatinine rise.
pearl: Best class for lowering triglycerides. Preferred over gemfibrozil for statin combination (gemfibrozil inhibits statin glucuronidation, raising myopathy risk).

name: Gemfibrozil
moa: PPAR-alpha agonist: upregulates lipoprotein lipase, enhances VLDL clearance, lowers triglycerides, and modestly raises HDL.
indications: Hypertriglyceridemia; mixed dyslipidemia (less favored due to interactions).
side_effects: Myopathy/rhabdomyolysis (esp. with statins), cholesterol gallstones, GI upset, hepatotoxicity.
pearl: Inhibits statin glucuronidation and OATP1B1, sharply raising statin levels and rhabdomyolysis risk; avoid with statins (use fenofibrate instead).

name: Niacin (Nicotinic acid)
moa: Inhibits hepatic VLDL/triglyceride synthesis and adipose lipolysis (reduces free fatty acid flux); raises HDL the most of any agent and lowers LDL and triglycerides.
indications: Historically for low HDL/mixed dyslipidemia; now rarely used (no added outcome benefit on top of statins).
side_effects: Cutaneous flushing/pruritus (prostaglandin-mediated), hyperglycemia, hyperuricemia (gout), hepatotoxicity.
pearl: Best agent for raising HDL. Flushing is prostaglandin-mediated—pretreat with aspirin to blunt it. Can worsen gout and glucose control.

name: Evolocumab
moa: Monoclonal antibody binds PCSK9, preventing it from degrading hepatic LDL receptors; more receptors recycle to the surface, dramatically lowering LDL.
indications: Familial hypercholesterolemia; ASCVD with inadequate LDL control on max statin; statin intolerance.
side_effects: Injection-site reactions, nasopharyngitis, myalgia; generally well tolerated.
pearl: Subcutaneous mAb giving very large LDL reductions on top of statins (FOURIER trial). Alirocumab is the other mAb in this class.

name: Alirocumab
moa: Human mAb against PCSK9; blocks LDL-receptor degradation so receptors recycle to the hepatocyte surface, sharply increasing LDL clearance.
indications: Heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia; ASCVD needing additional LDL lowering; statin-intolerant patients.
side_effects: Injection-site reactions, flu-like symptoms, myalgia, rare allergic reactions.
pearl: ODYSSEY OUTCOMES trial showed reduced cardiovascular events. Same target as evolocumab; dosed SC every 2–4 weeks.

name: Inclisiran
moa: siRNA that, via RISC, degrades hepatic PCSK9 mRNA and blocks PCSK9 translation; fewer LDL receptors are degraded, durably lowering LDL.
indications: Familial hypercholesterolemia; ASCVD as adjunct to statin for additional LDL lowering.
side_effects: Injection-site reactions, arthralgia; generally well tolerated.
pearl: Targets PCSK9 mRNA (vs antibodies targeting protein); dosed SC twice yearly after loading—major adherence advantage.

name: Bempedoic acid
moa: Prodrug activated only in the liver; inhibits ATP-citrate lyase upstream of HMG-CoA reductase, reducing cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors.
indications: Adjunct to statin, or for statin-intolerant patients, in familial hypercholesterolemia or ASCVD.
side_effects: Hyperuricemia/gout, tendon rupture, elevated creatinine, raised liver enzymes.
pearl: Liver-specific activation means no muscle toxicity—useful in statin intolerance. CLEAR Outcomes trial showed reduced cardiovascular events.

name: Icosapent ethyl
drug_class: Omega-3 fatty acid (purified EPA ethyl ester)
moa: Highly purified EPA reduces hepatic VLDL-triglyceride synthesis/secretion and enhances clearance; also has anti-inflammatory/antithrombotic effects.
indications: Severe hypertriglyceridemia; residual ASCVD risk reduction in patients with elevated TG on statins.
side_effects: Atrial fibrillation/flutter, increased bleeding risk, peripheral edema, dyspepsia.
pearl: REDUCE-IT showed cardiovascular event reduction (pure EPA, unlike mixed EPA/DHA products). Lowers TG without raising LDL.

name: Lomitapide
moa: Inhibits MTP in hepatocytes and enterocytes, blocking assembly of apo B–containing lipoproteins (VLDL, chylomicrons), markedly lowering LDL.
indications: Homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia (HoFH) as adjunct to other lipid-lowering therapy.
side_effects: Hepatic steatosis and transaminitis (boxed warning), GI/diarrhea, fat-soluble vitamin malabsorption.
pearl: Reserved for HoFH; hepatic fat accumulation is the signature toxicity—monitor LFTs. Mipomersen (apo B-100 antisense) is another HoFH agent.

name: Heparin (unfractionated)
moa: Binds antithrombin III, accelerating its inactivation of thrombin (IIa) and factor Xa (roughly equal effect on both).
indications: Acute: VTE/PE, ACS/MI, bridging, DIC. Anticoagulant of choice in pregnancy.
side_effects: Bleeding, HIT (type II), osteoporosis (long-term), hyperkalemia (aldosterone suppression).
pearl: Monitor with aPTT (intrinsic). Antidote = protamine sulfate. Short half-life; does not cross placenta.

name: Enoxaparin (LMWH)
moa: Binds antithrombin III; preferentially accelerates inhibition of factor Xa >> thrombin (IIa).
indications: VTE prophylaxis/treatment, ACS; outpatient/bridging use; preferred in pregnancy.
side_effects: Bleeding; lower HIT and osteoporosis risk than UFH; accumulates in renal failure.
pearl: No routine monitoring (follow anti-Xa if needed: obesity, renal, pregnancy). Protamine only partially reverses.

name: Fondaparinux
moa: Synthetic pentasaccharide; binds antithrombin III to selectively accelerate inhibition of factor Xa only.
indications: VTE prophylaxis/treatment; option for anticoagulation when HIT is a concern.
side_effects: Bleeding; contraindicated in severe renal impairment (CrCl <30).
pearl: Essentially no HIT risk (too small to form PF4 complexes). Not reversed by protamine.

name: Warfarin
drug_class: Anticoagulant - vitamin K epoxide reductase inhibitor (oral)
moa: Inhibits VKORC1, blocking gamma-carboxylation of factors II, VII, IX, X and proteins C & S.
indications: Long-term: AFib, mechanical heart valves, recurrent VTE.
side_effects: Bleeding, teratogenic (avoid in pregnancy), skin necrosis (early, esp. protein C deficiency).
pearl: Monitor with PT/INR (extrinsic). Reverse with vitamin K (slow) or 4-factor PCC/FFP (fast). Many CYP interactions.

name: Dabigatran
moa: Directly and reversibly inhibits free and clot-bound thrombin (factor IIa).
indications: Nonvalvular AFib, VTE treatment/prophylaxis.
side_effects: Bleeding, dyspepsia/GI upset; renally cleared (avoid in renal failure).
pearl: Antidote = idarucizumab (monoclonal antibody Fab). No routine monitoring.

name: Rivaroxaban
drug_class: Anticoagulant - direct factor Xa inhibitor (DOAC, oral)
moa: Directly and reversibly inhibits factor Xa (note the '-xaban' suffix).
indications: Nonvalvular AFib, VTE treatment/prophylaxis, post-op DVT prophylaxis.
side_effects: Bleeding; fewer drug/food interactions and no monitoring vs warfarin.
pearl: Antidote = andexanet alfa. No INR monitoring. Apixaban is preferred Xa inhibitor in renal disease.

name: Argatroban
moa: Directly inhibits thrombin (factor IIa); antithrombin-independent.
indications: Anticoagulation in patients with HIT (or HIT risk).
side_effects: Bleeding; hepatically cleared.
pearl: Used in HIT; hepatic clearance (use bivalirudin/renally cleared agent in liver failure). Monitor aPTT; falsely raises INR.

name: Aspirin
moa: Irreversibly acetylates COX-1, blocking platelet thromboxane A2 synthesis for the platelet lifespan (~7-10 days).
indications: ACS/MI, secondary prevention of stroke/CAD, post-stent (DAPT).
side_effects: GI bleed/ulcers, tinnitus (high dose), Reye syndrome in children, bronchospasm (AERD).
pearl: Prolongs bleeding time; normal PT/PTT. Antiplatelet effect outlasts the drug (irreversible).

name: Clopidogrel
moa: Irreversibly blocks platelet ADP (P2Y12) receptor, preventing GPIIb/IIIa activation and aggregation.
indications: ACS, post-coronary stent (with aspirin = DAPT), aspirin-intolerant patients.
side_effects: Bleeding; rarely TTP.
pearl: Prodrug requiring CYP2C19 activation - poor metabolizers (and some PPIs) reduce efficacy. Ticagrelor is reversible and not a prodrug.

name: Ticagrelor
moa: Reversibly, allosterically inhibits the platelet P2Y12 ADP receptor; not a prodrug (direct-acting).
indications: ACS, post-stent DAPT (with low-dose aspirin).
side_effects: Bleeding, dyspnea, bradycardia/ventricular pauses.
pearl: Faster onset than clopidogrel; high-dose aspirin (>100 mg) reduces efficacy. Dyspnea is the signature side effect.

name: Abciximab
moa: Monoclonal antibody Fab fragment blocking GP IIb/IIIa, the final common pathway of platelet aggregation (fibrinogen crosslinking).
indications: PCI/coronary angioplasty/stenting, ACS (IV).
side_effects: Bleeding, thrombocytopenia.
pearl: Blocks the FINAL common step of platelet aggregation. Small-molecule agents in class: eptifibatide, tirofiban.

name: Cilostazol
moa: Inhibits PDE3, raising platelet/vascular cAMP, causing vasodilation and inhibiting platelet aggregation.
indications: Intermittent claudication (peripheral artery disease).
side_effects: Headache, flushing, palpitations/tachycardia, GI upset/diarrhea.
pearl: Contraindicated in heart failure (oral PDE3 inhibitors increase mortality in HF). Dipyridamole is the other PDE inhibitor used as antiplatelet.

name: Alteplase (tPA)
moa: Converts plasminogen to plasmin, which degrades fibrin and dissolves existing thrombi.
indications: Acute ischemic stroke (<4.5h), massive/submassive PE, STEMI (when PCI unavailable).
side_effects: Hemorrhage (esp. intracranial), bleeding, angioedema.
