pearl: Reverse with aminocaproic acid/tranexamic acid (+ cryoprecipitate/FFP). Contraindicated with active bleeding, recent surgery, prior hemorrhagic stroke.

name: Tenecteplase
moa: Modified tPA: converts plasminogen to plasmin; greater fibrin specificity and longer half-life than alteplase.
indications: STEMI; increasingly used in acute ischemic stroke.
side_effects: Hemorrhage/bleeding (similar to alteplase).
pearl: Single IV bolus (vs alteplase infusion) due to long half-life; more fibrin-specific and more resistant to PAI-1.

name: Penicillin G
moa: Binds penicillin-binding proteins (transpeptidases), blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking; activates autolysins. Bactericidal, time-dependent.
indications: Syphilis (DOC), Strep pyogenes, viridans strep, N. meningitidis, Clostridium perfringens, Actinomyces.
side_effects: Hypersensitivity (rash to anaphylaxis), hemolytic anemia (Coombs+), seizures at high doses, interstitial nephritis.
pearl: DOC for syphilis; benzathine pen G IM for all stages. Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction after treating spirochetes.

name: Penicillin V
moa: Same as penicillin G — inhibits transpeptidase, blocking cell wall synthesis. Acid-stable, so orally bioavailable.
indications: Strep pharyngitis (Strep pyogenes), rheumatic fever prophylaxis.
side_effects: Hypersensitivity reactions, GI upset, rash.
pearl: The 'V' = oral form (acid-stable). First-line for strep pharyngitis and rheumatic fever secondary prophylaxis.

name: Nafcillin
moa: Bulky R group sterically blocks penicillinase (beta-lactamase) cleavage; inhibits PBP transpeptidase. Bactericidal.
indications: MSSA infections (cellulitis, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, bacteremia). Not for MRSA.
side_effects: Interstitial nephritis, hypersensitivity, neutropenia, extravasation tissue injury.
pearl: 'Use naf for staph.' Hepatically/biliary cleared (no renal dose adjustment). MRSA resistance via altered PBP2a (mecA).

name: Oxacillin
moa: Penicillinase-resistant beta-lactam; inhibits transpeptidase cross-linking. Bactericidal.
indications: MSSA infections — bacteremia, endocarditis, skin/soft tissue, osteomyelitis.
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity/transaminitis, interstitial nephritis, hypersensitivity.
pearl: Defines the susceptibility class: 'oxacillin-resistant S. aureus' = MRSA. Hepatically metabolized.

name: Dicloxacillin
moa: Penicillinase-resistant; inhibits PBP transpeptidase. Acid-stable for oral use.
indications: Oral MSSA skin/soft tissue infections (cellulitis, impetigo, mastitis), outpatient step-down.
side_effects: GI upset, hypersensitivity, rash, interstitial nephritis.
pearl: Oral antistaph option for outpatient MSSA cellulitis; take on empty stomach (food reduces absorption).

name: Methicillin
moa: Penicillinase-resistant beta-lactam inhibiting transpeptidase. Historical prototype, no longer clinically used.
indications: Historically MSSA; now obsolete (replaced by nafcillin/oxacillin).
side_effects: Acute interstitial nephritis (classic), hypersensitivity.
pearl: Classic cause of drug-induced acute interstitial nephritis. Lends its name to MRSA despite being off the market.

name: Amoxicillin
moa: Inhibits transpeptidase; amino group improves penetration through gram-negative porins. Bactericidal.
indications: Otitis media (first-line), strep pharyngitis, sinusitis, dental prophylaxis, H. pylori, early Lyme, enterococci, Listeria.
side_effects: Diarrhea, hypersensitivity, maculopapular rash if given in EBV/mono (non-allergic).
pearl: Greater oral bioavailability than ampicillin. Pseudo-allergic rash with concurrent EBV mononucleosis is classic.

name: Ampicillin
moa: Inhibits transpeptidase cross-linking; extended gram-negative coverage via porin penetration. Bactericidal.
indications: Listeria meningitis (DOC), enterococcal infections, susceptible E. coli/Proteus, group B strep prophylaxis.
side_effects: Diarrhea (more than amoxicillin), C. difficile colitis, rash, hypersensitivity.
pearl: 'Ampicillin/amoxicillin = HELPSS kill enterococci': H. influenzae, E. coli, Listeria, Proteus, Salmonella, Shigella, enterococci.

name: Amoxicillin-clavulanate
moa: Amoxicillin inhibits transpeptidase; clavulanate irreversibly inhibits beta-lactamase, restoring activity vs beta-lactamase producers.
indications: Sinusitis, otitis media (treatment failure), animal/human bite wounds, aspiration pneumonia, diabetic foot.
side_effects: Diarrhea (clavulanate-driven), cholestatic hepatitis, hypersensitivity, C. difficile.
pearl: DOC for dog/cat/human bites (covers Pasteurella, Eikenella, anaerobes). Clavulanate has no useful antibacterial activity alone.

name: Ampicillin-sulbactam
moa: Ampicillin inhibits PBPs; sulbactam inhibits beta-lactamase, extending coverage to beta-lactamase producers and anaerobes.
indications: Intra-abdominal/pelvic infections, aspiration pneumonia, diabetic foot, Acinetobacter (sulbactam has intrinsic activity).
side_effects: Diarrhea, rash, hypersensitivity, injection-site pain, C. difficile.
pearl: Sulbactam itself has activity against Acinetobacter baumannii. Good polymicrobial (aerobe + anaerobe) coverage.

name: Piperacillin-tazobactam
moa: Piperacillin inhibits transpeptidase with broad gram-neg/Pseudomonas coverage; tazobactam inhibits beta-lactamase.
indications: Nosocomial/HAP-VAP pneumonia, sepsis of unknown source, intra-abdominal infections, neutropenic fever, Pseudomonas.
side_effects: Hypokalemia, platelet dysfunction/bleeding, hypersensitivity, C. difficile; possible additive nephrotoxicity with vancomycin.
pearl: Workhorse empiric broad-spectrum agent ('pip-tazo'). Covers Pseudomonas; lacks reliable atypical/MRSA coverage.

name: Piperacillin
moa: Inhibits PBP transpeptidase; broad spectrum including Pseudomonas via good porin penetration. Bactericidal.
indications: Pseudomonas and broad gram-negative infections (almost always given combined with tazobactam clinically).
side_effects: Hypersensitivity, platelet dysfunction, hypokalemia, neurotoxicity at high doses.
pearl: Antipseudomonal penicillin; susceptible to beta-lactamase, so paired with tazobactam in practice.

name: Probenecid
moa: Inhibits renal organic anion transporter (OAT), blocking tubular secretion of penicillins to prolong serum levels.
indications: Adjunct to prolong/boost penicillin levels (e.g., neurosyphilis regimens); chronic gout.
side_effects: GI upset, rash, precipitates penicillin/cephalosporin toxicity; uric acid stones (with gout use).
pearl: Classic board fact: probenecid raises penicillin levels by blocking renal tubular secretion (OAT).

name: Aztreonam
moa: Binds PBP3 of gram-negative aerobes only; resistant to most beta-lactamases. No gram-positive or anaerobic activity.
indications: Gram-negative aerobic infections (incl. Pseudomonas) in penicillin-allergic patients or when avoiding aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity.
side_effects: Generally well tolerated; rare rash, GI upset, mild transaminitis.
pearl: Safe in penicillin-allergic patients — minimal cross-reactivity (except ceftazidime, shared side chain). Synergy with aminoglycosides.

name: Cefazolin
moa: Binds penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs), inhibiting transpeptidase-mediated peptidoglycan cross-linking; bactericidal cell-wall synthesis inhibitor.
indications: Surgical prophylaxis (drug of choice); skin/soft tissue (MSSA, strep); some E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus UTIs.
side_effects: Hypersensitivity, GI upset; low cross-reactivity with penicillins; no NMTT side chain, so no disulfiram-like reaction.
pearl: 1st-gen mnemonic PEcK: Proteus, E. coli, Klebsiella. Cefazolin = surgical prophylaxis of choice; does NOT cross the BBB.

name: Cefuroxime
moa: Inhibits PBPs to block peptidoglycan cross-linking; broader gram-negative coverage and greater beta-lactamase stability than 1st-gen.
indications: Community respiratory infections (H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis), sinusitis, otitis media, some gram-negatives.
side_effects: GI upset, hypersensitivity, rash; risk of C. difficile colitis.
pearl: 2nd-gen mnemonic HENS PEcK: + Haemophilus, Enterobacter, Neisseria, Serratia. 2nd-gens do NOT reliably penetrate CNS - not for meningitis.

name: Cefoxitin
moa: PBP inhibitor blocking cell-wall synthesis; notable anaerobic (B. fragilis) coverage among cephalosporins.
indications: Intra-abdominal and pelvic infections, mixed aerobic/anaerobic infections, surgical prophylaxis (colorectal).
side_effects: GI upset, hypersensitivity; cefoxitin lacks the NMTT side chain (disulfiram/bleeding effects are seen with cefotetan).
pearl: Cephamycins (cefoxitin, cefotetan) cover B. fragilis. Cefotetan has the NMTT group causing disulfiram-like reaction + hypoprothrombinemia.

name: Ceftriaxone
moa: Binds PBPs to inhibit transpeptidation; broad gram-negative coverage with strong CNS penetration.
indications: Meningitis, gonorrhea, Lyme disease (neuro/cardiac), pneumonia, pyelonephritis, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis.
side_effects: Biliary sludging/pseudocholelithiasis; kernicterus in neonates; rare immune hemolytic anemia.
pearl: Avoid in neonates (displaces bilirubin -> kernicterus; precipitates with Ca-containing IV fluids). Gonorrhea: pair with azithromycin/doxycycline.

name: Ceftazidime
moa: PBP inhibitor halting peptidoglycan cross-linking; the 3rd-gen agent with reliable Pseudomonas activity.
indications: Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections, febrile neutropenia, nosocomial gram-negative infections.
side_effects: Hypersensitivity, GI upset, C. difficile; selects for resistant organisms/ESBLs.
pearl: 3rd-gen with antipseudomonal activity. Shares an R1 side chain with aztreonam (cross-reactivity). Ceftazidime/avibactam covers many ESBL/KPC.

name: Cefepime
moa: Inhibits PBPs; zwitterion penetrates the gram-negative outer membrane rapidly and resists many beta-lactamases (incl. AmpC).
indications: Febrile neutropenia, Pseudomonas, nosocomial pneumonia, broad gram-positive + gram-negative empiric coverage.
side_effects: Neurotoxicity (encephalopathy, nonconvulsive status, myoclonus), especially in renal impairment; hypersensitivity.
pearl: Broadest cephalosporin coverage (gram+ AND antipseudomonal). Classic board fact: cefepime-induced neurotoxicity in renal failure - adjust dose.

name: Ceftaroline
moa: Binds PBP2a (the altered PBP in MRSA) in addition to standard PBPs, conferring anti-MRSA activity.
indications: MRSA skin/soft tissue infections and community-acquired pneumonia; broad gram-positive/gram-negative coverage.
side_effects: Hypersensitivity, GI upset, rash, rare neutropenia with prolonged use, C. difficile.
pearl: Only cephalosporin with MRSA coverage (binds PBP2a). Does NOT cover Pseudomonas - distinguishes it from cefepime/ceftazidime.

name: Vancomycin
moa: Binds D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursors, sterically blocking transglycosylation/transpeptidation; bactericidal (gram-positive only).
indications: MRSA, severe gram-positive infections, endocarditis; oral form for C. difficile colitis (not absorbed systemically).
side_effects: Nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, vancomycin infusion reaction ('red man syndrome'), DRESS, thrombocytopenia.
pearl: Resistance via D-Ala-D-Ala -> D-Ala-D-Lac (VRE). Infusion reaction is rate-dependent histamine release, NOT IgE allergy - slow infusion + antihistamine.

name: Meropenem
moa: Binds PBPs (esp. PBP2) to inhibit cell-wall synthesis; stable against most beta-lactamases including ESBLs.
indications: Severe/multidrug-resistant gram-negative infections, ESBL producers, polymicrobial intra-abdominal infections, meningitis.
side_effects: GI upset, hypersensitivity; lower seizure risk than imipenem; C. difficile, selection for CRE.
pearl: Broadest-spectrum beta-lactams ('last resort'). Meropenem preferred for CNS infections (lower seizure risk vs imipenem). Do NOT cover MRSA or atypicals.

name: Imipenem-cilastatin
moa: Imipenem inhibits PBPs blocking cell-wall synthesis; cilastatin inhibits renal dehydropeptidase-1 to prevent imipenem degradation and nephrotoxic metabolites.
indications: Serious gram-positive, gram-negative (incl. Pseudomonas), and anaerobic infections; ESBL producers.
side_effects: Seizures (highest carbapenem risk, esp. renal failure/CNS lesions), GI upset, hypersensitivity.
pearl: ALWAYS given with cilastatin to block renal DHP-1. Highest seizure potential of the carbapenems - avoid in seizure-prone patients.

name: Ertapenem
moa: PBP inhibitor blocking peptidoglycan cross-linking; long half-life allows once-daily dosing.
indications: Community-acquired intra-abdominal/pelvic infections, ESBL gram-negatives, diabetic foot infections.
side_effects: GI upset, hypersensitivity, infusion-site reactions, seizures (low risk), C. difficile.
pearl: Once-daily carbapenem but does NOT cover Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, or Enterococcus - the key gaps vs meropenem/imipenem.

name: Aztreonam
moa: Binds PBP3 of aerobic gram-negative bacteria only; inhibits cell-wall synthesis; no activity against gram-positives or anaerobes.
indications: Aerobic gram-negative infections (incl. Pseudomonas) in penicillin-allergic patients or to spare aminoglycosides.
side_effects: Generally well tolerated; GI upset, rash; minimal cross-reactivity with other beta-lactams.
pearl: Safe in penicillin/cephalosporin allergy - essentially no cross-reactivity (except ceftazidime, shared R1 side chain). Gram-negative aerobes ONLY.

name: Daptomycin
moa: Inserts lipophilic tail into gram-positive cell membrane (calcium-dependent), causing depolarization, K+ efflux, and rapid cell death.
indications: MRSA/VRE bacteremia, right-sided endocarditis, complicated skin/soft tissue infections.
side_effects: Myopathy/rhabdomyolysis (monitor CK), eosinophilic pneumonia; statins may increase myopathy risk.
pearl: INACTIVATED by pulmonary surfactant - do NOT use for pneumonia. Monitor CK weekly; hold statins if possible.

name: Telavancin
moa: Dual mechanism: binds D-Ala-D-Ala to inhibit peptidoglycan synthesis AND disrupts membrane potential/permeability via lipophilic side chain.
indications: Complicated MRSA skin/soft tissue infections; hospital-acquired/ventilator pneumonia when alternatives are unsuitable.
side_effects: Nephrotoxicity (boxed warning), QT prolongation, taste disturbance, foamy urine; teratogenic (boxed warning).
pearl: Vancomycin derivative with added membrane mechanism. Boxed warnings for nephrotoxicity and fetal harm - obtain pregnancy test before use.

name: Erythromycin
moa: Binds 50S ribosomal subunit (23S rRNA), blocks translocation of peptidyl-tRNA; bacteriostatic.
indications: Atypical pneumonia (Mycoplasma, Legionella, Chlamydia), pertussis, gram-positive infx in penicillin-allergic pts.
side_effects: GI upset (motilin agonist), QT prolongation, cholestatic hepatitis (estolate), reversible deafness.
pearl: Potent CYP3A4 inhibitor -> raises warfarin, statin, theophylline levels. Used as prokinetic for gastroparesis.

name: Azithromycin
moa: Binds 50S subunit (23S rRNA), inhibits translocation; bacteriostatic. Long half-life from tissue accumulation.
indications: CAP/atypicals, Chlamydia (1 g single dose), MAC prophylaxis/treatment, gonorrhea co-therapy, COPD exacerbation.
side_effects: GI upset, QT prolongation, ototoxicity, rare cardiovascular death in high-risk pts.
pearl: Minimal CYP3A4 interaction (vs erythromycin/clarithromycin). Z-pack once-daily; concentrates intracellularly.

name: Clarithromycin
moa: Binds 50S subunit (23S rRNA), blocks translocation; bacteriostatic.
indications: H. pylori triple therapy, CAP/atypicals, MAC, strep pharyngitis in PCN-allergic.
side_effects: GI upset, metallic taste, QT prolongation, hepatotoxicity. Teratogenic (avoid in pregnancy).
pearl: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitor. Classic component of H. pylori regimen (PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin).

name: Doxycycline
moa: Binds 30S subunit, blocks aminoacyl-tRNA binding to A site; bacteriostatic.
indications: Rickettsia/RMSF, Lyme, Chlamydia, Mycoplasma, CA-MRSA skin, acne, malaria prophylaxis, anthrax.
side_effects: Photosensitivity, GI upset, esophagitis, tooth discoloration/bone growth inhibition in kids.
pearl: Safe in renal failure (eliminated in feces, not renal) - the tetracycline of choice in CKD. Drug of choice for RMSF even in children.

name: Tetracycline
moa: Binds 30S subunit, prevents aminoacyl-tRNA binding to A site; bacteriostatic.
indications: H. pylori (quadruple therapy), acne, Chlamydia, atypicals; largely supplanted by doxycycline.
side_effects: Tooth discoloration, photosensitivity, GI upset, Fanconi syndrome (expired drug).
pearl: Absorption blocked by divalent/trivalent cations (Ca, Mg, Fe, antacids, dairy) -> chelation. Contraindicated in pregnancy and <8 yo.

name: Minocycline
moa: Binds 30S subunit, inhibits aminoacyl-tRNA binding to A site; bacteriostatic. High CNS penetration.
indications: Acne, CA-MRSA, meningococcal carrier eradication, rheumatoid arthritis adjunct.
side_effects: Vestibular toxicity (dizziness, vertigo), blue-gray skin/mucosa pigmentation, drug-induced lupus, DRESS.
pearl: Vestibular side effects distinguish it from other tetracyclines; classic cause of slate-gray skin pigmentation.

name: Tigecycline
moa: Tetracycline derivative; binds 30S subunit blocking aminoacyl-tRNA, evades efflux/ribosomal protection resistance; bacteriostatic.
indications: Complicated intra-abdominal/skin infections, MDR organisms (MRSA, VRE, ESBL, Acinetobacter).
side_effects: Severe nausea/vomiting, increased all-cause mortality (black box), pancreatitis, photosensitivity.
pearl: Reserved for MDR infections. Poor blood/urine levels -> NOT for bacteremia or UTI. Broad including anaerobes.

name: Gentamicin
moa: Binds 30S subunit, causes mRNA misreading + blocks initiation complex; bactericidal, concentration-dependent. O2-dependent uptake.
indications: Serious gram-negative (incl. Pseudomonas) infections, synergy with beta-lactams for enterococcal/staph endocarditis.
side_effects: Nephrotoxicity (ATN, reversible), ototoxicity (often irreversible), neuromuscular blockade.
pearl: Synergistic with beta-lactams (cell-wall agent enhances uptake). Ineffective vs anaerobes (need O2 for uptake). Once-daily dosing reduces toxicity.

name: Tobramycin
moa: Binds 30S subunit causing mRNA misreading; bactericidal, concentration-dependent.
indications: Pseudomonas infections; inhaled form for cystic fibrosis airway colonization; gram-negative sepsis.
side_effects: Nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity (cochlear + vestibular), neuromuscular blockade.
pearl: Slightly better anti-Pseudomonas activity than gentamicin; inhaled tobramycin is a mainstay in CF management.

name: Amikacin
moa: Binds 30S subunit, induces mRNA misreading; bactericidal. Resistant to most aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes.
indications: Multidrug-resistant gram-negatives, resistant TB (2nd-line), nosocomial gram-negative sepsis.
side_effects: Nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, neuromuscular blockade.
pearl: Broadest aminoglycoside; least affected by bacterial inactivating enzymes -> reserved for resistant organisms.

name: Clindamycin
moa: Binds 50S subunit (23S rRNA), blocks translocation (overlaps macrolide site); bacteriostatic.
indications: Anaerobes ABOVE the diaphragm (lung abscess, aspiration pneumonia), CA-MRSA, toxin suppression in nec fasc/TSS.
side_effects: C. difficile pseudomembranous colitis (classic), diarrhea, rash.
pearl: Inhibits toxin production (add-on in necrotizing fasciitis/strep TSS). Inducible resistance detected by D-test. Top board cause of C. diff colitis.

name: Linezolid
moa: Binds 23S rRNA of 50S subunit, prevents formation of 70S initiation complex; bacteriostatic (cidal vs strep).
indications: VRE, MRSA (incl. pneumonia/skin), drug-resistant gram-positives; full oral bioavailability.
side_effects: Thrombocytopenia/myelosuppression (prolonged use), peripheral & optic neuropathy, serotonin syndrome.
pearl: Reversible nonselective MAO inhibitor -> serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs; avoid tyramine-rich foods. No cross-resistance with other classes.

name: Chloramphenicol
moa: Binds 50S subunit, inhibits peptidyltransferase (blocks peptide bond formation); bacteriostatic.
indications: Rare in US; meningitis/RMSF/typhoid when alternatives contraindicated; broad-spectrum reserve.
side_effects: Dose-dependent reversible anemia; idiosyncratic dose-INDEPENDENT aplastic anemia; gray baby syndrome.
pearl: Gray baby syndrome in neonates from immature UDP-glucuronosyltransferase. Aplastic anemia limits use. Inhibits CYP450.

name: Quinupristin-dalfopristin
moa: Both bind 50S subunit at distinct sites (dalfopristin then quinupristin), synergistically blocking elongation; bactericidal.
indications: VRE (E. faecium, NOT E. faecalis), complicated gram-positive skin infections.
side_effects: Arthralgias/myalgias (prominent), infusion-site reactions/phlebitis, hyperbilirubinemia.
pearl: Covers E. faecium but intrinsically inactive against E. faecalis - classic distinguishing fact. Strong CYP3A4 inhibitor.

name: Ciprofloxacin
moa: Inhibits DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II; primary gram-neg target) and topoisomerase IV, blocking DNA replication; bactericidal
indications: Gram-negative infections incl. Pseudomonas, complicated UTI/pyelonephritis, GI infections, anthrax prophylaxis
side_effects: Tendon rupture (esp. Achilles), QT prolongation, GI upset, CNS effects, photosensitivity, dysglycemia
pearl: Best antipseudomonal oral FQ; strong CYP1A2 inhibitor (raises theophylline/caffeine). Separate from cations/dairy (chelation).

name: Levofloxacin
moa: Inhibits DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, halting bacterial DNA replication; bactericidal
indications: Community-acquired pneumonia, sinusitis, complicated UTI/pyelonephritis; covers S. pneumoniae and atypicals
side_effects: Tendinopathy/rupture, QT prolongation, peripheral neuropathy, CNS effects, aortic aneurysm/dissection risk, dysglycemia
pearl: Active L-isomer of ofloxacin; classic 'respiratory FQ' for atypicals (Legionella, Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila).

name: Moxifloxacin
moa: Dual inhibition of DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV; enhanced gram-positive/anaerobic coverage; bactericidal
indications: CAP, intra-abdominal infections, MDR-TB regimens; broad gram-positive + anaerobic activity
side_effects: Greatest QT prolongation of the FQs, tendon rupture, hepatotoxicity, CNS effects, dysglycemia
pearl: Largely hepatically cleared - NOT for UTIs (poor urine levels). Highest QT risk in the class.

name: Sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim (TMP-SMX)
moa: SMX inhibits dihydropteroate synthase (PABA analog); TMP inhibits dihydrofolate reductase - sequential folate blockade
indications: PCP (Pneumocystis) treatment/prophylaxis, UTIs, MRSA skin infections, Nocardia, toxoplasmosis
side_effects: Stevens-Johnson syndrome, hyperkalemia, hemolysis in G6PD def., myelosuppression, crystalluria, photosensitivity
pearl: First-line for PCP and Nocardia. TMP raises K+/creatinine (blocks ENaC + tubular Cr secretion). Avoid near term (kernicterus).

name: Sulfadiazine
moa: PABA analog inhibiting dihydropteroate synthase, blocking bacterial folate (and thus nucleic acid) synthesis
indications: Toxoplasmosis (with pyrimethamine), nocardiosis, rheumatic fever prophylaxis
side_effects: Crystalluria/nephrotoxicity, SJS/TEN, hemolysis in G6PD deficiency, photosensitivity, myelosuppression
pearl: Sulfadiazine + pyrimethamine = standard toxoplasma encephalitis Rx; hydrate/alkalinize urine to prevent crystalluria.

name: Sulfasalazine
moa: Colonic bacteria cleave it into sulfapyridine (carrier) + 5-ASA, which acts locally as anti-inflammatory on the bowel
indications: Ulcerative colitis, Crohn disease, rheumatoid arthritis (DMARD)
side_effects: Reversible oligospermia, GI upset, hemolysis (G6PD), photosensitivity, sulfa hypersensitivity reactions
pearl: Classic cause of reversible male infertility (oligospermia). 5-ASA is the active anti-inflammatory moiety.

name: Silver sulfadiazine
moa: Releases silver ions that disrupt bacterial cell membranes/DNA, plus sulfonamide folate inhibition; topical antibacterial
indications: Prevention of infection in 2nd/3rd-degree burns
side_effects: Local irritation/discoloration, transient leukopenia/neutropenia, sulfa hypersensitivity
pearl: Go-to topical for burn wound prophylaxis; avoid in sulfa allergy and near term/neonates. Watch transient neutropenia early on.

name: Metronidazole
moa: Reduced by anaerobes/protozoa to reactive nitro radicals that fragment microbial DNA; bactericidal
indications: Anaerobic infections, C. difficile colitis, bacterial vaginosis, Trichomonas, Giardia, amebiasis, H. pylori
side_effects: Metallic taste, disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol, peripheral neuropathy, dose-related CNS toxicity (encephalopathy/seizures)
pearl: DISULFIRAM-LIKE REACTION with alcohol is the classic board fact. Anaerobes 'below the diaphragm'; GET GAP coverage.

name: Tinidazole
moa: Anaerobic/protozoal reduction to nitro radical intermediates that break microbial DNA strands
indications: Trichomoniasis, giardiasis, amebiasis, bacterial vaginosis
side_effects: Metallic taste, disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol, GI upset, headache
pearl: Longer half-life than metronidazole = shorter/single-dose courses; same alcohol-avoidance warning.

name: Nitrofurantoin
moa: Reduced by bacterial nitroreductases to reactive intermediates that damage ribosomal proteins, DNA, and metabolism
indications: Uncomplicated lower UTI/cystitis; UTI prophylaxis (incl. pregnancy, except at term)
side_effects: Acute/chronic pulmonary toxicity (pneumonitis/fibrosis), hemolysis in G6PD def., hepatotoxicity, neuropathy, brown urine
pearl: Concentrates in urine - NOT for pyelonephritis/systemic infection. Ineffective if CrCl <30-60; avoid at term and in neonates.

name: Pyrimethamine
moa: Inhibits protozoal dihydrofolate reductase, blocking folate-dependent nucleic acid synthesis
indications: Toxoplasmosis (with sulfadiazine), malaria treatment/prophylaxis combinations
side_effects: Megaloblastic anemia, bone marrow suppression, GI upset (folate antagonism)
pearl: Give LEUCOVORIN (folinic acid) to rescue host marrow without reversing antiprotozoal effect. Pairs with sulfadiazine.

name: Trimethoprim
moa: Inhibits bacterial dihydrofolate reductase, blocking DHF-to-THF conversion and folate-dependent synthesis
indications: UTIs (alone or as TMP-SMX), PCP prophylaxis component
side_effects: Megaloblastic anemia, leukopenia, hyperkalemia, raised creatinine (blocks tubular Cr secretion)
pearl: Hyperkalemia is classic (blocks ENaC like amiloride). Leucovorin/folinic acid reverses the megaloblastic anemia.

name: Delafloxacin
moa: Dual inhibition of DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV; enhanced potency in acidic environments; bactericidal
indications: Acute bacterial skin/skin-structure infections and CAP, including MRSA coverage
side_effects: Class FQ effects: tendinopathy, QT prolongation (lower than others), GI upset, C. difficile diarrhea
pearl: One of few FQs with reliable MRSA activity; anionic structure boosts uptake in acidic abscess milieu.

name: Ofloxacin
moa: Inhibits DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV, halting bacterial DNA replication; bactericidal
indications: UTIs, gonorrhea/chlamydia (historical), otic/ophthalmic infections, prostatitis
side_effects: Tendon rupture, QT prolongation, photosensitivity, CNS effects, GI upset
pearl: Racemic parent of levofloxacin; topical otic/ophthalmic forms widely used for ear and eye infections.

name: Amphotericin B
moa: Binds ergosterol in fungal membrane, forming pores that cause K+/Mg2+ leakage and cell death
indications: Serious systemic mycoses: cryptococcal meningitis (with flucytosine), invasive aspergillosis, mucormycosis, severe Candida/Histoplasma
side_effects: Infusion fever/chills/rigors, dose-dependent nephrotoxicity, hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia, anemia, phlebitis
pearl: "Amphoterrible": nephrotoxic. Liposomal form reduces toxicity. Hydrate + supplement K/Mg. Premedicate to blunt infusion rxn.

name: Nystatin
moa: Same as amphotericin: binds ergosterol forming membrane pores; too toxic for IV use
indications: Oral candidiasis (thrush) as "swish and swallow," cutaneous/diaper-area candidiasis, vaginal candidiasis
side_effects: Minimal systemic (not absorbed orally); local GI upset, bad taste, nausea
pearl: Topical/oral only because too toxic for systemic use; "swish and swallow" for oral thrush.

name: Fluconazole
moa: Inhibits fungal CYP450 14-alpha-demethylase, blocking conversion of lanosterol to ergosterol
indications: Cryptococcal meningitis maintenance/prophylaxis in HIV, candidiasis (oral/esophageal/vaginal/systemic), coccidioidomycosis
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity, QT prolongation, CYP inhibition (drug interactions), GI upset; less endocrine effect than ketoconazole
pearl: Penetrates CSF well, water-soluble, renally cleared -> DOC for cryptococcal meningitis maintenance. Azoles are teratogenic CYP inhibitors.

name: Itraconazole/Voriconazole
moa: Inhibit fungal CYP450 14-alpha-demethylase, blocking conversion of lanosterol to ergosterol
indications: Voriconazole = DOC for invasive aspergillosis; itraconazole for dimorphic fungi (Blastomyces, Histoplasma, Sporothrix)
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity, QT prolongation; voriconazole -> visual/color disturbances, photosensitivity, periostitis; itraconazole -> negative inotrope (avoid in CHF)
pearl: Voriconazole = transient visual disturbances ("seeing colors") is classic. Voriconazole DOC for invasive Aspergillus.

name: Caspofungin
moa: Inhibits beta-(1,3)-D-glucan synthase -> disrupts fungal cell wall synthesis
indications: Invasive candidiasis (incl. azole-resistant C. glabrata, C. krusei), salvage therapy for invasive aspergillosis
side_effects: Generally well tolerated; flushing (histamine release), elevated LFTs, GI upset
pearl: "-fungin" = echinocandin; targets cell WALL (glucan), not membrane. Not effective vs Cryptococcus.

name: Terbinafine
moa: Inhibits squalene epoxidase -> blocks ergosterol synthesis; toxic squalene accumulation is fungicidal
indications: Dermatophytoses, especially onychomycosis (nail fungus) and tinea infections
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity (monitor LFTs), GI upset, taste disturbance/loss (dysgeusia), rare neutropenia
pearl: Oral DOC for onychomycosis. Acts EARLIER in ergosterol pathway than azoles (squalene epoxidase, not CYP).

name: Griseofulvin
moa: Disrupts microtubule/mitotic spindle, inhibiting fungal mitosis; deposits in keratin-containing tissue
indications: Dermatophytoses of skin, hair, and nails (tinea); largely replaced by terbinafine
side_effects: Disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol, CYP450 inducer (warfarin/OCP failure), teratogenic, photosensitivity, hepatotoxicity
pearl: Deposits in newly formed keratin. CYP inducer + disulfiram-like rxn. Avoid in porphyria/pregnancy.

name: Flucytosine
moa: Converted by fungal cytosine deaminase to 5-FU -> inhibits thymidylate synthase (DNA synthesis) and disrupts RNA
indications: Cryptococcal meningitis and severe systemic candidiasis, combined with amphotericin B (synergy, prevents resistance)
side_effects: Bone marrow suppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia), GI upset, hepatotoxicity
pearl: Combined with amphotericin B; rapid resistance if used alone. Myelosuppression from conversion to 5-FU.

name: Acyclovir
moa: Monophosphorylated by viral thymidine kinase, then host kinases to triphosphate -> inhibits viral DNA polymerase + chain terminator
indications: HSV-1/2 (encephalitis, genital herpes, mucocutaneous), VZV (chickenpox, shingles); valacyclovir = prodrug with better bioavailability
side_effects: Crystalline nephropathy (obstructive, prevent with hydration), neurotoxicity (esp. in renal failure), generally well tolerated
pearl: Needs viral thymidine kinase to activate. Resistance = mutated/absent viral TK -> use foscarnet. Hydrate to prevent crystal nephropathy.

name: Ganciclovir
moa: Monophosphorylated by CMV kinase UL97, then host kinases -> triphosphate inhibits viral DNA polymerase
indications: CMV retinitis/colitis/pneumonitis, esp. in AIDS and transplant patients; valganciclovir = oral prodrug
side_effects: Bone marrow suppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia) - more toxic than acyclovir; nephrotoxicity
pearl: DOC for CMV. Myelosuppression is dose-limiting (worse with zidovudine). Resistance via UL97 mutation -> foscarnet.

name: Foscarnet
moa: Directly binds the pyrophosphate-binding site of viral DNA polymerase/reverse transcriptase; NO kinase activation needed
indications: CMV retinitis and acyclovir-resistant HSV/VZV (TK-mutant strains); ganciclovir-resistant CMV
side_effects: Nephrotoxicity, electrolyte abnormalities (hypo/hyper-calcemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia) -> seizures
pearl: "FOScarnet = pyroFOSphate analog." No viral kinase needed -> works on TK-mutant resistant strains. Causes Ca/Mg derangements + seizures.

name: Oseltamivir
moa: Inhibits influenza neuraminidase -> prevents release/spread of progeny virions from infected cells
indications: Treatment and prophylaxis of influenza A and B; most effective within 48 hours of symptom onset
side_effects: Nausea/vomiting (GI upset), headache; rare neuropsychiatric events
pearl: Active vs both influenza A and B (unlike amantadine = A only). Start <48h of symptoms. Zanamivir = inhaled alternative.

name: Zidovudine (AZT)
moa: Phosphorylated to active triphosphate -> competitively inhibits HIV reverse transcriptase + chain terminator (lacks 3'-OH)
indications: HIV (part of combination ART); classic for prevention of mother-to-child (vertical) HIV transmission
side_effects: Bone marrow suppression (megaloblastic anemia, neutropenia), lactic acidosis, myopathy, headache; class effect: lipodystrophy
pearl: Used in pregnancy to prevent perinatal HIV transmission. NRTI class -> lactic acidosis + hepatic steatosis (mito toxicity).

name: Ritonavir
moa: Inhibits HIV-1 protease -> prevents cleavage of gag-pol polyprotein -> immature, noninfectious virions
indications: HIV combination therapy; low-dose ritonavir used as pharmacokinetic "booster" of other PIs
side_effects: Hyperglycemia/insulin resistance, lipodystrophy (fat redistribution), hyperlipidemia, GI intolerance; potent CYP3A4 inhibitor
pearl: Potent CYP3A4 inhibitor -> used as a "booster." "-navir tease a protease." PI class -> lipodystrophy + hyperglycemia. Indinavir -> nephrolithiasis.

name: Isoniazid (INH)
moa: Activated by mycobacterial catalase-peroxidase (KatG); inhibits InhA (enoyl-ACP reductase), blocking mycolic acid synthesis for the cell wall.
indications: Active TB (combo); first-line for latent TB monotherapy (9 mo, '9H').
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, sideroblastic anemia, drug-induced lupus, B6 depletion; high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis + seizures in overdose.
pearl: Give B6 (pyridoxine) to prevent neuropathy. Refractory INH-overdose seizures treated with high-dose pyridoxine. KatG or inhA mutation = resistance.

name: Rifampin
moa: Inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase (binds beta subunit, rpoB), blocking mRNA transcription.
indications: Active TB (combo); latent TB (4 mo monotherapy); meningococcal & H. influenzae type b prophylaxis; leprosy; prosthetic/biofilm staph infections.
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity, orange-red body fluids (urine, tears, sweat), flu-like syndrome, thrombocytopenia, GI upset.
pearl: Potent CYP450 INDUCER (lowers warfarin, OCPs, protease inhibitors, methadone). Rifabutin = less induction, preferred in HIV. rpoB mutation = resistance.

name: Pyrazinamide (PZA)
moa: Converted by pyrazinamidase (pncA) to pyrazinoic acid; disrupts membrane energetics/coenzyme A synthesis; most active in acidic intracellular/caseous environment.
indications: Active TB during the 2-month intensive phase (combo).
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity (dose-dependent), hyperuricemia (gout flares/arthralgias), photosensitivity, GI upset.
pearl: Key sterilizing drug in acidic milieu; enabled shortening to 6-mo regimens. Inhibits renal urate excretion -> hyperuricemia. pncA mutation = resistance.

name: Ethambutol
moa: Inhibits arabinosyl transferase (embB), blocking arabinogalactan polymerization in the mycobacterial cell wall.
indications: Active TB (combo, intensive phase) — added to prevent emergence of resistance.
side_effects: Dose- and duration-dependent optic neuritis: decreased visual acuity and RED-GREEN color blindness (usually reversible if caught early).
pearl: Classic board answer for color-vision loss/optic neuritis. Get baseline + periodic eye exam. Renally cleared (dose-adjust in renal failure). embB mutation = resistance.

name: Streptomycin
moa: Binds 30S ribosomal subunit, causing mRNA misreading and inhibiting protein synthesis; bactericidal.
indications: Active TB (injectable alternative, esp. drug-resistant disease or when a first-line oral agent isn't tolerated).
side_effects: Nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity (vestibular & cochlear), neuromuscular blockade; teratogenic (irreversible fetal ototoxicity/deafness).
pearl: Historic first effective TB drug. Resistance via rpsL or rrs (16S rRNA) mutations. Avoid in pregnancy. Unlike other aminoglycosides, still mainly a TB-niche drug.

name: Rifabutin
moa: Inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase (rpoB), same mechanism as rifampin.
indications: TB and MAC prophylaxis/treatment in HIV patients on antiretrovirals (preferred over rifampin); MAC disease.
side_effects: Uveitis (esp. with strong CYP inhibitors), neutropenia, orange fluids, polyarthralgia, GI upset; less hepatotoxic than rifampin.
pearl: Preferred over rifampin in HIV: WEAKER CYP3A4 inducer, fewer interactions with protease inhibitors/NNRTIs. Uveitis is the distinguishing toxicity.

name: Rifapentine
moa: Inhibits bacterial DNA-dependent RNA polymerase (rpoB), like other rifamycins.
indications: Latent TB (once-weekly with INH x12 wk, '3HP'); active drug-susceptible TB (4-mo rifapentine + moxifloxacin regimen).
side_effects: Orange-red fluids, hepatotoxicity, flu-like reactions, GI upset; CYP3A4 induction.
pearl: Long half-life enables once-weekly/short-course therapy, improving adherence. Backbone of the 3HP latent-TB and 4-month active-TB regimens.

name: Bedaquiline
moa: Inhibits mycobacterial ATP synthase (binds atpE/c subunit of F1F0 ATP synthase), depleting ATP; bactericidal against replicating and dormant bacilli.
indications: Multidrug-resistant TB (MDR/pre-XDR/XDR), as part of the all-oral BPaL/BPaLM regimens.
side_effects: QT PROLONGATION, hepatotoxicity, headache, nausea; very long terminal half-life (months).
pearl: First novel-mechanism TB drug in ~40 years. Black-box warning for QT prolongation and an excess-mortality signal; monitor ECG. Cornerstone of modern MDR-TB therapy.

name: Linezolid
moa: Binds 23S rRNA of the 50S subunit, preventing formation of the 70S initiation complex; inhibits protein synthesis (bacteriostatic).
indications: MDR/XDR-TB (component of BPaL/BPaLM); also resistant gram-positive infections (VRE, MRSA).
side_effects: Myelosuppression (thrombocytopenia, anemia), peripheral & optic neuropathy (prolonged use), serotonin syndrome, lactic acidosis.
pearl: Reversible nonselective MAO inhibitor -> serotonin syndrome risk with SSRIs/tyramine. Long-term TB use limited by neuropathy/cytopenias (often dose-reduced).

name: Pretomanid
moa: Activated by deazaflavin-dependent nitroreductase (Ddn); inhibits mycolic acid synthesis (aerobic killing) and releases reactive nitrogen species/NO (anaerobic killing).
indications: Drug-resistant pulmonary TB (XDR or treatment-intolerant/nonresponsive MDR) only within BPaL/BPaLM (with bedaquiline + linezolid +/- moxifloxacin).
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, QT prolongation, myelosuppression (largely regimen/linezolid-related), male reproductive toxicity (animal data).
pearl: Approved only as part of fixed BPaL/BPaLM combos, never as monotherapy. Dual aerobic + anaerobic killing helped shorten drug-resistant TB therapy to ~6 months.

name: Moxifloxacin
moa: Inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II); in gram-negatives also topoisomerase IV — blocks DNA replication/supercoiling.
indications: MDR-TB (core component); part of the 4-month rifapentine + moxifloxacin regimen for drug-susceptible TB.
side_effects: QT prolongation, tendinopathy/rupture, CNS effects, dysglycemia, aortic aneurysm/dissection risk; hepatotoxicity.
pearl: Later-generation fluoroquinolones (moxi/levofloxacin) are the backbone of MDR-TB regimens. Resistance via gyrA mutations. Separate from chelating cations (Ca/Mg/Al/Fe) which impair absorption.

name: Capreomycin
moa: Inhibits protein synthesis by binding the ribosome/rRNA and blocking translocation; bactericidal.
indications: MDR-TB as an injectable second-line agent (alternative to aminoglycosides).
side_effects: Nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia).
pearl: Injectable second-line agent; cross-resistance can occur with aminoglycosides (via rrs/tlyA). Largely replaced by oral bedaquiline-based regimens (WHO now deprioritizes injectables).

name: Ethionamide
moa: Prodrug activated by EthA; inhibits InhA -> blocks mycolic acid synthesis (same target as isoniazid).
indications: MDR-TB (second-line combination therapy).
side_effects: GI upset, hepatotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, hypothyroidism/goiter, gynecomastia, metallic taste.
pearl: Structural analog of INH (both hit InhA); inhA-mediated cross-resistance with INH can occur. Distinctive toxicity: hypothyroidism. Give pyridoxine to limit neuropathy.

name: Dapsone
moa: Inhibits dihydropteroate synthase, blocking folate synthesis (like sulfonamides); bacteriostatic against M. leprae.
indications: Leprosy (multidrug therapy with rifampin +/- clofazimine); PCP prophylaxis/treatment; dermatitis herpetiformis.
side_effects: Hemolysis (esp. G6PD deficiency), METHEMOGLOBINEMIA, agranulocytosis, peripheral neuropathy, dapsone hypersensitivity (DRESS).
pearl: Check G6PD before starting. Methemoglobinemia is the classic toxicity -> antidote is methylene blue (which is ineffective/risky in G6PD deficiency).

name: Fluoxetine
moa: Selectively inhibits presynaptic serotonin (5-HT) reuptake transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic 5-HT.
indications: MDD, GAD, OCD, panic disorder, bulimia, PMDD; first-line for depression.
side_effects: Sexual dysfunction, GI upset, insomnia, headache, hyponatremia (SIADH), initial anxiety/jitteriness.
pearl: Longest half-life SSRI (active metabolite norfluoxetine) - least discontinuation syndrome. Potent CYP2D6/3A4 inhibitor; FDA-approved in peds.

name: Sertraline
moa: Selective inhibition of 5-HT reuptake; weak dopamine reuptake inhibition at high doses; minimal NE effect.
indications: MDD, OCD, panic disorder, PTSD, social anxiety, PMDD.
side_effects: GI distress (most diarrhea), sexual dysfunction, insomnia, hyponatremia.
pearl: Often preferred SSRI post-MI/cardiac disease and in breastfeeding; highest rate of GI/diarrhea among SSRIs.

name: Paroxetine
moa: Selective 5-HT reuptake inhibition; also weak anticholinergic activity and weak NE reuptake inhibition.
indications: MDD, GAD, OCD, panic disorder, social anxiety, PTSD.
side_effects: Most sedating/anticholinergic SSRI, weight gain, sexual dysfunction, worst discontinuation syndrome.
pearl: Short half-life + no active metabolite + potent CYP2D6 inhibition -> worst withdrawal; teratogenic (cardiac defects) - avoid in pregnancy.

name: Citalopram
moa: Highly selective 5-HT reuptake inhibition (racemic mixture of R/S-enantiomers).
indications: MDD; anxiety disorders (off-label).
side_effects: Sexual dysfunction, hyponatremia, dose-dependent QT prolongation.
pearl: FDA max dose 40 mg (20 mg if >60 yo, hepatic impairment, or CYP2C19 poor metabolizers) due to QT prolongation; escitalopram is the active S-enantiomer.

name: Venlafaxine
moa: Inhibits 5-HT reuptake at low doses; adds NE (and weak DA) reuptake inhibition at higher doses.
indications: MDD, GAD, panic disorder, social anxiety; useful with comorbid neuropathic pain.
side_effects: Dose-dependent diastolic hypertension, nausea, sweating, severe discontinuation syndrome.
pearl: Monitor BP at higher doses; short half-life -> notorious discontinuation; desvenlafaxine is its active metabolite.

name: Duloxetine
moa: Inhibits both 5-HT and NE reuptake across the dose range (5-HT effect tends to predominate).
indications: MDD, GAD, diabetic peripheral neuropathy, fibromyalgia, chronic musculoskeletal pain.
side_effects: Nausea, dry mouth, hypertension, hepatotoxicity (avoid in liver disease/heavy alcohol use).
pearl: Strong choice when depression coexists with neuropathic/chronic pain; contraindicated in significant hepatic impairment.

name: Amitriptyline
moa: Blocks 5-HT and NE reuptake; also antagonizes muscarinic (M1), histamine (H1), and alpha-1 adrenergic receptors.
indications: MDD (rarely first-line), neuropathic pain, migraine prophylaxis, fibromyalgia, chronic pain.
side_effects: Anticholinergic (dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation), sedation, orthostasis, weight gain, QT prolongation.
pearl: TCA overdose = the 3 C's: Cardiotoxicity (wide QRS, arrhythmia), Convulsions, Coma; treat with IV sodium bicarbonate.

name: Nortriptyline
moa: Preferentially inhibits NE reuptake (also 5-HT); fewer off-target receptor effects than tertiary TCAs.
indications: MDD, neuropathic pain; preferred TCA in the elderly.
side_effects: Anticholinergic and cardiac effects (less than amitriptyline), orthostasis, sedation.
pearl: Best-tolerated TCA in the elderly (least anticholinergic/orthostasis); active metabolite of amitriptyline; has a therapeutic window (levels monitored).

name: Clomipramine
moa: Most serotonergic TCA - strong 5-HT reuptake inhibition plus NE; also antimuscarinic/antihistaminic/anti-alpha-1.
indications: OCD (TCA of choice for OCD); MDD.
side_effects: Anticholinergic effects, sedation, weight gain, seizures (dose-related), sexual dysfunction.
pearl: The TCA used for OCD; among the most likely TCAs to lower the seizure threshold.

name: Phenelzine
moa: Irreversibly inhibits MAO-A and MAO-B, increasing 5-HT, NE, and DA.
indications: Atypical and treatment-resistant depression (third-line).
side_effects: Orthostatic hypotension, weight gain, sexual dysfunction, insomnia; hypertensive crisis, serotonin syndrome.
pearl: Tyramine foods (aged cheese, cured meats, wine) -> hypertensive crisis; 2-wk washout to/from serotonergic drugs (5 wk for fluoxetine).

name: Selegiline
drug_class: MAOI (selective MAO-B at low dose; transdermal for MDD)
moa: Selectively inhibits MAO-B at low oral doses; transdermal patch also inhibits brain MAO-A while largely sparing gut MAO-A.
indications: MDD (transdermal patch); Parkinson disease (oral, as adjunct).
side_effects: Application-site reaction (patch), insomnia, orthostasis; dietary tyramine risk at higher doses.
pearl: Lowest patch dose (6 mg/24h) needs no tyramine restriction; higher doses lose MAO-B selectivity and require dietary precautions.

name: Bupropion
moa: Inhibits reuptake of NE and DA; negligible serotonergic activity.
indications: MDD, seasonal affective disorder, smoking cessation (Zyban); ADHD (off-label).
side_effects: Insomnia, dry mouth, weight loss, lowered seizure threshold; minimal sexual dysfunction.
pearl: No weight gain or sexual side effects; contraindicated in seizure disorders and bulimia/anorexia (electrolyte-related seizure risk).

name: Mirtazapine
moa: Blocks presynaptic alpha-2 autoreceptors (increasing NE and 5-HT release); antagonizes 5-HT2, 5-HT3, and H1 receptors.
indications: MDD, especially with insomnia, anorexia/weight loss, or in cachectic/elderly patients.
side_effects: Sedation, marked weight gain/increased appetite, dry mouth; agranulocytosis (rare).
pearl: Sedation is MORE pronounced at LOW doses (H1 effect dominates; added NE at higher doses offsets it); minimal sexual dysfunction/GI upset.

name: Trazodone
moa: Antagonizes 5-HT2A and weakly inhibits 5-HT reuptake; strong H1 and alpha-1 antagonism.
indications: Insomnia (primary use, low dose); MDD (high doses, less common).
side_effects: Sedation, orthostatic hypotension, priapism.
pearl: Classic association: PRIAPISM (alpha-1 blockade - a urologic emergency); mainly used as a sleep aid at low doses.

name: Haloperidol
moa: High-affinity dopamine D2 receptor antagonist throughout CNS pathways; antipsychotic effect via mesolimbic blockade.
indications: Schizophrenia (positive sx), acute agitation/delirium, Tourette syndrome, acute mania.
side_effects: High EPS (acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia), NMS, QT prolongation, hyperprolactinemia.
pearl: Highest EPS risk; long-acting IM decanoate depot for adherence. IV use carries notable QT/torsades risk.

name: Chlorpromazine
moa: D2 receptor antagonist; also blocks muscarinic (M1), histamine (H1), and alpha-1 adrenergic receptors.
indications: Schizophrenia, intractable hiccups, nausea/vomiting.
side_effects: Anticholinergic effects, sedation, orthostatic hypotension; corneal/lens deposits, blue-gray skin discoloration, photosensitivity.
pearl: Low potency = more anticholinergic/antihistamine/anti-alpha effects, less EPS. Classic: corneal/lens deposits.

name: Fluphenazine
moa: Potent D2 receptor antagonist in CNS dopaminergic pathways.
indications: Chronic schizophrenia, especially nonadherent patients (depot form).
side_effects: High EPS, tardive dyskinesia, hyperprolactinemia, NMS.
pearl: Long-acting IM decanoate depot improves adherence; high-potency typical with marked EPS like haloperidol.

name: Thioridazine
moa: D2 antagonist with strong anticholinergic and anti-adrenergic (alpha-1) activity.
indications: Schizophrenia (now rarely used due to cardiac risk).
side_effects: Marked QT prolongation, retinitis pigmentosa (irreversible at high doses), anticholinergic effects.
pearl: Classic: retinal deposits / retinitis pigmentosa ('thioRIDAZINE = RETINA'). Boxed warning for QT prolongation.

name: Clozapine
moa: Antagonist at 5-HT2A and D4 with low-affinity (loose) D2 blockade; weak D2 binding explains minimal EPS.
indications: Treatment-resistant schizophrenia; reduces suicidality in schizophrenia.
side_effects: Agranulocytosis, lowers seizure threshold (dose-dependent), myocarditis, weight gain/metabolic syndrome, hypersalivation, orthostasis, constipation/ileus.
pearl: Mandatory ANC monitoring (REMS). 'Watch the C's': agranulocytosis, Convulsions/seizures, myoCarditis, Constipation.

name: Risperidone
moa: Potent 5-HT2A and D2 receptor antagonist.
indications: Schizophrenia, bipolar mania, irritability in autism.
side_effects: Hyperprolactinemia (most of the atypicals), dose-dependent EPS, weight gain, QT prolongation.
pearl: Highest prolactin elevation among atypicals (galactorrhea, gynecomastia, amenorrhea). Long-acting injectable available.

name: Olanzapine
moa: 5-HT2A and D2 receptor antagonist with broad receptor (M1, H1, alpha) activity.
indications: Schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, acute agitation (IM), adjunct in treatment-resistant depression.
side_effects: Significant weight gain, dyslipidemia, hyperglycemia/diabetes; worst metabolic profile along with clozapine.
pearl: Greatest metabolic/weight-gain risk (with clozapine). Monitor weight, glucose, lipids. IM not with parenteral benzo (excess sedation/hypotension).

name: Quetiapine
moa: 5-HT2A and D2 antagonist (low D2 occupancy); strong H1 antagonism drives sedation.
indications: Schizophrenia, bipolar disorder (mania & depression), adjunct in depression, off-label insomnia.
side_effects: Marked sedation, orthostatic hypotension, weight gain, metabolic effects; low EPS/prolactin.
pearl: Lowest EPS/prolactin risk - preferred (with clozapine/pimavanserin) for Parkinson disease psychosis. Very sedating via H1 blockade.

name: Ziprasidone
moa: 5-HT2A and D2 receptor antagonist.
indications: Schizophrenia, bipolar mania, acute agitation (IM).
side_effects: QT prolongation (most of the atypicals), relatively weight/metabolic neutral.
pearl: Most QT prolongation among atypicals - baseline ECG. Weight-neutral; must be taken with food (doubles absorption).

name: Aripiprazole
moa: Partial agonist at D2 and 5-HT1A; antagonist at 5-HT2A ('dopamine system stabilizer').
indications: Schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, adjunct in major depression, irritability in autism.
side_effects: Akathisia, insomnia/activation, nausea; low weight gain/metabolic risk, low prolactin.
pearl: Unique D2 partial agonist. Favorable metabolic profile; impulse-control disorders (gambling, hypersexuality) reported.

name: Lithium
moa: Inhibits inositol monophosphatase (depletes inositol/IP3 signaling) and GSK-3 beta; precise mechanism unclear.
indications: Bipolar disorder (acute mania & maintenance), reduces suicide risk; augmentation in MDD.
side_effects: Tremor, nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, hypothyroidism, weight gain, leukocytosis; Ebstein anomaly in pregnancy.
pearl: Narrow therapeutic index (~0.6-1.2 mEq/L); renally cleared - NSAIDs, thiazides, ACEi raise levels. Toxicity mnemonic LMNOP.

name: Valproate (valproic acid / divalproex)
moa: Increases GABA, blocks voltage-gated Na+ channels; modulates T-type Ca2+ currents.
indications: Bipolar mania (esp. mixed/rapid-cycling), seizures (broad spectrum), migraine prophylaxis.
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis, thrombocytopenia, hyperammonemia, weight gain, tremor, alopecia; teratogenic (neural tube defects).
pearl: Most teratogenic mood stabilizer (NTDs/spina bifida) - avoid in pregnancy. CYP inhibitor; raises lamotrigine levels.

name: Lamotrigine
moa: Blocks voltage-gated Na+ channels, inhibiting glutamate release.
indications: Bipolar maintenance (best for depressive episodes), seizures.
side_effects: Stevens-Johnson syndrome / TEN (titrate slowly), benign rash, dizziness, diplopia, ataxia.
pearl: Slow titration prevents SJS; valproate inhibits its glucuronidation (~doubles level, halve dose). Best for bipolar DEPRESSION.

name: Carbamazepine
moa: Blocks voltage-gated Na+ channels, stabilizing neuronal membranes.
indications: Bipolar mania, focal seizures, trigeminal neuralgia.
side_effects: Agranulocytosis, aplastic anemia, SIADH/hyponatremia, SJS/TEN (HLA-B*1502), ataxia, diplopia; teratogen (NTDs).
pearl: Potent CYP450 inducer (auto-induces own metabolism). Test HLA-B*1502 in Asian patients for SJS risk (HLA-A*3101 broader).

name: Diazepam
moa: Positive allosteric modulator at GABA-A receptor; increases FREQUENCY of Cl- channel opening, enhancing GABA-mediated hyperpolarization
indications: Status epilepticus, alcohol withdrawal, muscle spasm/spasticity, anxiety, preop sedation
side_effects: Sedation, anterograde amnesia, ataxia, dependence/tolerance, respiratory depression (esp. with alcohol/opioids)
pearl: Prototype long-acting BZD with active metabolites (e.g. desmethyldiazepam, oxazepam); flumazenil reverses; BZDs increase FREQUENCY, barbiturates increase DURATION

name: Lorazepam
moa: GABA-A positive allosteric modulator; increases frequency of Cl- channel opening
indications: Status epilepticus (first-line), alcohol withdrawal, anxiety, agitation, chemo nausea
side_effects: Sedation, respiratory depression, amnesia, dependence, falls in elderly
pearl: LOT drugs (Lorazepam, Oxazepam, Temazepam) cleared only by glucuronidation (no CYP/phase I) - safest BZDs in liver disease and elderly

name: Midazolam
moa: GABA-A positive allosteric modulator; rapid onset, highly lipophilic
indications: Procedural/conscious sedation, induction of anesthesia, status epilepticus (IM/intranasal)
side_effects: Respiratory depression (dose-related, esp. with opioids), hypotension, sedation
pearl: Water-soluble in vial (open ring at acidic pH), becomes lipophilic at physiologic pH - fast CNS entry; DOC for procedural sedation

name: Alprazolam
moa: GABA-A positive allosteric modulator; rapid onset, high potency
indications: Panic disorder, generalized anxiety disorder
side_effects: High abuse/dependence potential, severe interdose rebound anxiety, withdrawal seizures
pearl: Highest dependence/abuse liability among BZDs due to rapid onset and high potency; notorious for difficult withdrawal

name: Triazolam
moa: GABA-A positive allosteric modulator; very short half-life
indications: Short-term treatment of insomnia (sleep-onset)
side_effects: Anterograde amnesia, rebound insomnia, daytime anxiety, complex sleep behaviors
pearl: CYP3A4 substrate - levels dangerously raised by strong inhibitors (azoles, macrolides, grapefruit), causing accumulation

name: Chlordiazepoxide
moa: GABA-A positive allosteric modulator; long half-life with active metabolites
indications: Alcohol withdrawal (CIWA-guided), anxiety
side_effects: Sedation, prolonged effect, ataxia, accumulation in elderly/liver disease
pearl: Classic agent for alcohol detox; long half-life of parent + active metabolites gives smooth, self-tapering withdrawal coverage

name: Flumazenil
moa: Competitive antagonist at the BZD binding site on GABA-A receptor
indications: Reversal of BZD overdose/oversedation, reversal of procedural sedation
side_effects: Can precipitate acute withdrawal SEIZURES in BZD-dependent patients; resedation (short half-life)
pearl: Antidote for BZD toxicity; AVOID in chronic users and in mixed/TCA overdose (removes protective BZD effect, unmasks seizures)

name: Phenobarbital
moa: GABA-A positive allosteric modulator; increases DURATION of Cl- channel opening; at high doses directly opens Cl- channel (GABA-independent)
indications: Neonatal/refractory seizures, status epilepticus (refractory), sedation
side_effects: Sedation, respiratory depression, dependence, low therapeutic index, potent CYP450 INDUCER
pearl: Barbiturates increase DURATION; can open Cl- channel without GABA at high doses (no ceiling, more lethal than BZDs); strong P450 inducer; no specific antidote

name: Thiopental
moa: GABA-A modulator (increases duration of Cl- channel opening); highly lipophilic with rapid brain entry, terminated by redistribution
indications: Induction of anesthesia (historical), rapid control of ICP
side_effects: Respiratory/cardiac depression, hypotension, laryngospasm
pearl: Effect terminated by REDISTRIBUTION to skeletal muscle/fat, not metabolism; high lipid solubility = rapid onset and brief duration

name: Zolpidem
moa: Acts at the BZD site of GABA-A receptors, selective for the alpha-1 (BZ1/omega-1) subunit
indications: Insomnia (sleep-onset; CR form for maintenance)
side_effects: Complex sleep behaviors (sleep-driving/eating), next-day impairment, dependence (less than BZDs)
pearl: Selective alpha-1 binding = mainly hypnotic with minimal anxiolytic/anticonvulsant/myorelaxant effect; reversed by flumazenil; short half-life, less daytime grogginess

name: Zaleplon
moa: Acts at BZD site, alpha-1-preferring GABA-A agonist; ultra-short half-life
indications: Insomnia (sleep-onset, including middle-of-night awakening)
side_effects: Minimal next-day sedation, dizziness, rare complex sleep behaviors
pearl: Shortest-acting z-drug (~1 hr half-life) - can be dosed for middle-of-the-night awakening with little morning hangover

name: Eszopiclone
moa: Acts at BZD site of GABA-A receptor (z-drug); longer half-life than other z-drugs
indications: Insomnia (sleep-onset and sleep-maintenance)
side_effects: Unpleasant/metallic taste (dysgeusia, classic), next-day sedation, headache
pearl: Longest-acting z-drug - good for sleep MAINTENANCE; hallmark side effect is bitter metallic taste; S-enantiomer of zopiclone

name: Buspirone
moa: Partial agonist at serotonin 5-HT1A receptors; NO action on GABA-A
indications: Generalized anxiety disorder (chronic)
side_effects: Dizziness, headache, nausea; NO sedation, NO dependence, NO respiratory depression
pearl: Non-sedating, no abuse potential, no additive CNS depression with alcohol; delayed onset 1-2 weeks (not for acute/PRN anxiety); avoid combining with MAOIs

name: Ramelteon
moa: Selective agonist at melatonin MT1 and MT2 receptors in the suprachiasmatic nucleus
indications: Insomnia (sleep-onset), especially circadian/elderly
side_effects: Dizziness, fatigue, elevated prolactin; minimal abuse potential
pearl: No GABA activity = no dependence, no abuse, NOT a controlled substance; safe alternative in patients with substance abuse history

name: Phenytoin
moa: Blocks voltage-gated Na+ channels, prolonging inactivated state and reducing high-frequency repetitive firing.
indications: Focal and generalized tonic-clonic seizures; status epilepticus (IV fosphenytoin preferred).
side_effects: Gingival hyperplasia, hirsutism, nystagmus, ataxia, sedation, megaloblastic anemia, osteopenia, DRESS/SJS.
pearl: Zero-order (saturable) kinetics; potent CYP inducer; fetal hydantoin syndrome; HLA-B*1502 raises SJS risk in Asians.

name: Carbamazepine
moa: Blocks voltage-gated Na+ channels, stabilizing inactivated state to limit repetitive neuronal firing.
indications: Focal seizures (first-line), generalized tonic-clonic; trigeminal neuralgia (DOC); bipolar disorder.
side_effects: Diplopia, ataxia, SIADH/hyponatremia, agranulocytosis, aplastic anemia, SJS/TEN, hepatotoxicity, teratogen.
pearl: Strong CYP3A4 inducer (autoinduction); test HLA-B*1502 in Asians (SJS/TEN); teratogen (neural tube defects). Can worsen absence/myoclonic.

name: Valproic acid
moa: Blocks Na+ channels, increases GABA (inhibits GABA transaminase), and blocks T-type Ca2+ channels.
indications: Broad-spectrum: focal, tonic-clonic, absence, myoclonic seizures; bipolar disorder; migraine prophylaxis.
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis, weight gain, tremor, alopecia, thrombocytopenia, hyperammonemia, teratogen.
pearl: Most teratogenic AED — neural tube defects; avoid in pregnancy/childbearing age. CYP inhibitor; inhibits lamotrigine metabolism (raises level).

name: Lamotrigine
moa: Blocks voltage-gated Na+ channels and inhibits glutamate release; stabilizes neuronal membranes.
indications: Focal and generalized seizures, absence, Lennox-Gastaut; bipolar maintenance (depression).
side_effects: SJS/TEN (titration-related), rash, dizziness, diplopia, headache, insomnia.
pearl: Titrate slowly to avoid life-threatening rash; valproate raises its level (titrate even slower); relatively safe in pregnancy.

name: Levetiracetam
moa: Binds synaptic vesicle protein SV2A, modulating neurotransmitter release; not a classic channel blocker.
indications: Focal, generalized tonic-clonic, myoclonic seizures; status epilepticus; broad-spectrum first-line.
side_effects: Irritability, mood/behavioral changes, depression, somnolence, fatigue.
pearl: No CYP interactions, renal clearance, no level monitoring — popular first-line. Key adverse effect is neuropsychiatric (rage/depression).

name: Ethosuximide
moa: Blocks thalamic T-type voltage-gated Ca2+ channels, suppressing the 3-Hz spike-wave rhythm of absence seizures.
indications: Drug of choice for absence (petit mal) seizures only.
side_effects: GI distress, fatigue, headache; rarely SJS, agranulocytosis.
pearl: First-line for absence (equal efficacy but fewer cognitive/attentional effects vs valproate (Glauser 2010); both superior to lamotrigine); ineffective for tonic-clonic. Mnemonic: EThosuximide = T-type.

name: Phenobarbital
moa: Binds GABA-A receptor, increasing duration of Cl- channel opening, enhancing GABAergic inhibition.
indications: Neonatal seizures (first-line), refractory status epilepticus, focal/tonic-clonic in resource-limited settings.
side_effects: Sedation, respiratory depression, tolerance/dependence, cognitive impairment, paradoxical hyperactivity in children.
pearl: First-line for neonatal seizures; potent CYP inducer; barbiturates increase DURATION of Cl- channel opening (vs benzos = frequency).

name: Diazepam
moa: Binds GABA-A receptor, increasing frequency of Cl- channel opening, potentiating GABAergic inhibition.
indications: Status epilepticus (acute abortive), acute repetitive seizures (rectal/IV); alcohol withdrawal.
side_effects: Sedation, respiratory depression, dependence/tolerance, anterograde amnesia.
pearl: Abortive for status epilepticus (lorazepam IV preferred for longer duration). Antidote: flumazenil. Benzos increase FREQUENCY of Cl- opening.

name: Gabapentin
moa: Binds α2δ subunit of voltage-gated Ca2+ channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release; GABA analog (no GABA activity).
indications: Focal seizures (adjunct); neuropathic pain, postherpetic neuralgia, restless legs syndrome.
side_effects: Sedation, dizziness, ataxia, peripheral edema, weight gain.
pearl: Renally cleared, no CYP interactions; despite name does not act on GABA receptors. Mainly used for neuropathic pain now; misuse potential.

name: Topiramate
moa: Blocks Na+ channels, potentiates GABA-A, antagonizes AMPA/kainate glutamate receptors, inhibits carbonic anhydrase.
indications: Focal and generalized seizures, Lennox-Gastaut; migraine prophylaxis; weight loss (with phentermine).
side_effects: Cognitive slowing/word-finding difficulty, weight loss, paresthesias, kidney stones, acute glaucoma, oligohidrosis, metabolic acidosis.
pearl: Carbonic anhydrase inhibition causes kidney stones + metabolic acidosis; nicknamed 'dope-a-max' for cognitive dulling; teratogenic (cleft lip/palate).

name: Vigabatrin
moa: Irreversibly inhibits GABA transaminase, increasing brain GABA concentrations.
indications: Infantile spasms (West syndrome); refractory focal seizures (adjunct).
side_effects: Irreversible peripheral visual field loss (constriction), MRI abnormalities, sedation, weight gain.
pearl: First-line for infantile spasms (esp. tuberous sclerosis); permanent vision loss mandates regular visual field testing (REMS program).

name: Lacosamide
moa: Selectively enhances SLOW inactivation of voltage-gated Na+ channels (distinct from classic fast-inactivation blockers).
indications: Focal seizures (mono/adjunct); IV available for status epilepticus.
side_effects: Dizziness, diplopia, ataxia, PR-interval prolongation, headache.
pearl: Enhances slow (not fast) Na+ channel inactivation; check ECG — dose-dependent PR prolongation; useful IV option in status epilepticus.

name: Oxcarbazepine
moa: Prodrug; active metabolite (licarbazepine) blocks voltage-gated Na+ channels, stabilizing the inactivated state.
indications: Focal seizures (mono/adjunct), generalized tonic-clonic; better tolerated alternative to carbamazepine.
side_effects: Hyponatremia (more than carbamazepine), dizziness, diplopia, sedation, rash (cross-reactivity).
pearl: No epoxide metabolite, so fewer interactions/less autoinduction vs carbamazepine; watch sodium. HLA-B*1502 still relevant.

name: Tiagabine
moa: Selectively inhibits the GAT-1 GABA transporter, blocking neuronal/glial GABA reuptake to prolong inhibition.
indications: Focal seizures (adjunct therapy).
side_effects: Dizziness, asthenia, nervousness, tremor; can provoke seizures/status in non-epileptics.
pearl: Mnemonic: TIAgabine blocks GABA reuptake (GAT-1); off-label use risks seizures in patients without epilepsy.

name: Morphine
drug_class: Strong opioid agonist (phenanthrene); prototype mu-receptor agonist
moa: Agonist at mu (and kappa/delta) opioid GPCRs; opens K+ / closes Ca2+ channels, hyperpolarizing neurons, inhibiting pain transmission
indications: Severe acute/chronic pain, MI pain, acute pulmonary edema, palliative dyspnea
side_effects: Respiratory depression, miosis, constipation, sedation, hypotension, urinary retention, pruritus (histamine), tolerance/dependence
pearl: Toxic triad = pinpoint pupils + respiratory depression + CNS depression/coma. Active metabolite M6G accumulates in renal failure. Reverse with naloxone.

name: Fentanyl
moa: Highly potent, lipophilic full mu-opioid agonist; rapid CNS penetration; short duration after single dose due to redistribution
indications: Anesthesia/procedural analgesia, transdermal chronic pain, breakthrough cancer pain, epidural/spinal analgesia
side_effects: Respiratory depression, chest wall (truncal) rigidity, bradycardia, sedation; minimal histamine release
pearl: ~100x more potent than morphine; leading cause of overdose deaths (often as illicit adulterant). Rigid-chest with rapid IV push. Patch absorption increases with heat/fever.

name: Methadone
drug_class: Synthetic long-acting mu-opioid agonist + NMDA receptor antagonist
moa: Full mu agonist with long, variable half-life; also antagonizes NMDA receptors and inhibits monoamine reuptake (aids neuropathic pain)
indications: Opioid use disorder maintenance/detox, chronic pain, neuropathic pain
side_effects: QT prolongation/torsades, delayed respiratory depression, sedation, constipation; accumulation due to long t1/2
pearl: Dose-dependent QT prolongation - get baseline ECG. Long, variable half-life means delayed respiratory depression and accumulation risk; dispensed for OUD via licensed clinics.

name: Codeine
moa: Prodrug O-demethylated by CYP2D6 to morphine (active analgesic); antitussive via medullary cough center
indications: Mild-moderate pain, cough suppression (antitussive)
side_effects: Constipation, nausea, sedation, respiratory depression; same opioid class effects
pearl: CYP2D6 pharmacogenetics: ultra-rapid metabolizers get morphine toxicity (contraindicated post-tonsillectomy in kids and in breastfeeding). Poor metabolizers get little analgesia.

name: Oxycodone
moa: Full mu-opioid agonist with high oral bioavailability; metabolized by CYP3A4/CYP2D6 (active independent of CYP2D6 conversion)
indications: Moderate-severe pain (often combined with acetaminophen); chronic pain (extended-release)
side_effects: Constipation, sedation, respiratory depression, nausea, high abuse/dependence potential
pearl: OxyContin (ER) central to the opioid epidemic; reformulated to deter crushing. Reliable analgesia even in CYP2D6 poor metabolizers (unlike codeine/tramadol).

name: Meperidine
moa: Mu-opioid agonist with anticholinergic/local-anesthetic properties; metabolite normeperidine is a CNS stimulant/proconvulsant
indications: Acute pain, post-op shivering (largely fallen out of favor)
side_effects: Seizures/tremor (normeperidine accumulation, esp. renal failure), tachycardia, mydriasis (anticholinergic), serotonin syndrome
pearl: Serotonin syndrome with MAOIs (and SSRIs) - classic contraindication. Anticholinergic effects cause tachycardia/mydriasis rather than classic miosis. Largely avoided now.

name: Tramadol
drug_class: Weak/atypical opioid agonist + monoamine reuptake inhibitor
moa: Weak mu-opioid agonist (parent + CYP2D6-active metabolite) PLUS inhibits serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake
indications: Moderate pain, neuropathic pain, when wanting lower opioid potency
side_effects: Seizures (lowers threshold), serotonin syndrome, nausea, dizziness, dependence
pearl: Dual mechanism = seizure risk + serotonin syndrome (caution with SSRIs/SNRIs/TCAs/MAOIs). Analgesia partly CYP2D6-dependent. Avoid in seizure disorders.

name: Buprenorphine
moa: High-affinity partial mu agonist with slow dissociation and ceiling effect on respiratory depression; displaces full agonists
indications: Opioid use disorder (often as buprenorphine/naloxone, Suboxone), chronic pain
side_effects: Precipitated withdrawal if given too soon after full agonist, constipation, headache; ceiling limits respiratory depression
pearl: Ceiling effect = safer in overdose, but high affinity makes overdose hard to reverse (needs high-dose naloxone). Naloxone added to deter IV abuse (inactive sublingually).

name: Pentazocine
drug_class: Mixed agonist-antagonist opioid (kappa agonist / weak mu partial agonist-antagonist)
moa: Kappa-opioid agonist with weak mu antagonism/partial agonism
indications: Moderate pain (limited use)
side_effects: Dysphoria/hallucinations (kappa), can precipitate withdrawal in opioid-dependent patients, increased cardiac workload
pearl: Giving to a patient on a full mu agonist precipitates withdrawal (mu antagonism). Kappa activation causes dysphoria - distinguishing feature of mixed agonist-antagonists.

name: Naloxone
moa: Competitive antagonist at mu (and kappa/delta) opioid receptors; displaces agonists
indications: Opioid overdose reversal (IV/IM/intranasal)
side_effects: Acute opioid withdrawal (tachycardia, hypertension, agitation, pain, rarely pulmonary edema), short duration requiring redosing
pearl: Antidote for opioid overdose. Short half-life < many opioids (esp. methadone/fentanyl) - patient may re-narcotize, requires monitoring/repeat dosing. Poor oral bioavailability (first-pass).

name: Naltrexone
moa: Competitive mu-opioid receptor antagonist with long duration of action; orally active
indications: Alcohol use disorder (reduces craving/reward), opioid use disorder relapse prevention (after detox)
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity (high dose), precipitated withdrawal if opioids on board, nausea, headache
pearl: Used for BOTH alcohol and opioid dependence. Patient must be opioid-free (7-10 days) before starting or it precipitates withdrawal. Long-acting vs short-acting naloxone.

name: Acetaminophen
moa: Central inhibition of COX (peroxide-sensitive pathway) and analgesia/antipyresis in CNS; minimal peripheral anti-inflammatory effect
indications: Mild-moderate pain, fever; preferred analgesic in pregnancy, peptic ulcer, children (no Reye risk)
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity in overdose; minimal GI/renal/platelet effects at therapeutic doses
pearl: Overdose forms toxic NAPQI that depletes glutathione causing centrilobular (zone III) hepatic necrosis. Antidote = N-acetylcysteine (replenishes glutathione). NOT anti-inflammatory.

name: N-acetylcysteine
moa: Replenishes hepatic glutathione stores and provides sulfhydryl groups to detoxify NAPQI
indications: Acetaminophen overdose/hepatotoxicity; also mucolytic; contrast-induced nephropathy prophylaxis
side_effects: Anaphylactoid reaction (IV), nausea/vomiting, rash, bronchospasm; rotten-egg smell (PO)
pearl: Antidote for acetaminophen toxicity - most effective within 8-10 hrs of ingestion. Plot level on Rumack-Matthew nomogram to guide treatment timing.

name: Loperamide
moa: Activates mu-opioid receptors in the GI myenteric plexus, slowing motility; P-glycoprotein substrate keeps it out of CNS
indications: Symptomatic treatment of diarrhea
side_effects: Constipation; in massive overdose/abuse: QT prolongation and cardiac arrhythmias (torsades), CNS opioid effects
pearl: Does not cross BBB at normal doses (no analgesia/euphoria) due to P-gp efflux. Abused in high doses for euphoria/withdrawal - causes cardiotoxicity (QT prolongation/torsades).

name: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid)
moa: Irreversibly acetylates COX-1/COX-2; blocks platelet thromboxane A2 (TXA2) and prostaglandin synthesis for the platelet's 7-10 day lifespan.
indications: Antiplatelet (ACS, post-MI, ischemic stroke/TIA prevention), analgesia, antipyresis, anti-inflammatory.
side_effects: GI ulcers/bleeding, tinnitus (high dose), hypersensitivity/bronchospasm, Reye syndrome in children.
pearl: Overdose: early respiratory alkalosis, then mixed metabolic acidosis. Avoid in kids with viral illness (Reye). Treat overdose with bicarbonate to alkalinize urine.

name: Ibuprofen
drug_class: Nonselective COX inhibitor (propionic acid NSAID); reversible
moa: Reversibly inhibits COX-1 and COX-2, decreasing prostaglandin synthesis (analgesic, antipyretic, anti-inflammatory).
indications: Pain, fever, inflammation, dysmenorrhea, acute gout.
side_effects: GI bleed/ulcers, renal impairment, fluid retention/HTN, can blunt aspirin's antiplatelet effect.
pearl: Reversible (unlike aspirin); taking it before aspirin competitively blocks aspirin's access to platelet COX-1, reducing cardioprotection.

name: Indomethacin
moa: Potent reversible COX-1/COX-2 inhibitor reducing prostaglandins; closes PDA by removing PGE2-mediated patency.
indications: Closure of patent ductus arteriosus (neonates), acute gout, ankylosing spondylitis.
side_effects: Marked GI toxicity, headache, renal effects; CNS effects more common than other NSAIDs.
pearl: Classic agent to CLOSE a PDA; PGE1 (alprostadil) keeps the ductus OPEN.

name: Ketorolac
drug_class: Nonselective COX inhibitor (acetic acid NSAID, parenteral)
moa: Reversible COX inhibitor with strong analgesic potency comparable to opioids for short-term pain.
indications: Short-term (<5 days) moderate-severe acute pain, postoperative analgesia.
side_effects: High GI bleeding/ulcer risk, acute kidney injury; use limited to ~5 days.
pearl: Opioid-sparing analgesia, but renal/GI toxicity caps duration at 5 days.

name: Celecoxib
moa: Selectively inhibits COX-2 (inducible, inflammation) while sparing COX-1 (gastric mucosa, platelet TXA2).
indications: Osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, pain in patients at high GI-bleed risk.
side_effects: Increased thrombotic/cardiovascular risk; less GI toxicity than nonselective NSAIDs.
pearl: Spares COX-1, so platelet TXA2 is intact while COX-2-derived endothelial prostacyclin (PGI2) is suppressed, raising prothrombotic/CV risk. Sulfonamide allergy caution.

name: Acetaminophen (paracetamol)
moa: Weak/central COX inhibition; reduces pain and fever but has essentially no peripheral anti-inflammatory action.
indications: Pain and fever; preferred when NSAIDs contraindicated (PUD, gastritis, kids with viral illness).
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity in overdose (centrilobular necrosis); minimal GI/renal/antiplatelet effect.
pearl: Overdose generates toxic NAPQI that depletes hepatic glutathione. Antidote: N-acetylcysteine (replenishes glutathione).

name: Colchicine
moa: Binds tubulin, blocking microtubule polymerization, impairing neutrophil chemotaxis/migration and NLRP3 inflammasome activation.
indications: Acute gout flare, prophylaxis, familial Mediterranean fever, pericarditis.
side_effects: Severe GI: diarrhea, nausea, vomiting (dose-limiting); myelosuppression, neuromyopathy.
pearl: GI distress (diarrhea) is the hallmark dose-limiting toxicity; useful when NSAIDs/steroids are contraindicated.

name: Allopurinol
moa: Inhibits xanthine oxidase, decreasing conversion of hypoxanthine/xanthine to uric acid (lowers urate production).
indications: Chronic gout prophylaxis, tumor lysis syndrome prevention, recurrent urate stones.
side_effects: Hypersensitivity (incl. SJS/TEN, esp. HLA-B*58:01), rash, may precipitate acute flare on initiation.
pearl: Inhibits xanthine oxidase metabolism of azathioprine/6-MP, raising their levels, so reduce their doses. Do NOT start during an acute flare.

name: Febuxostat
moa: Selective non-purine inhibitor of xanthine oxidase, decreasing uric acid synthesis.
indications: Chronic gout in patients intolerant of or refractory to allopurinol.
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity (monitor LFTs), increased cardiovascular mortality risk; flare on initiation.
pearl: Alternative when allopurinol fails/causes hypersensitivity; carries a CV death warning vs allopurinol. Same azathioprine/6-MP interaction.

name: Probenecid
moa: Inhibits URAT1 (and OAT) in the proximal tubule, blocking uric acid reabsorption and increasing renal urate excretion.
indications: Chronic gout in undersecretors of uric acid (not acute flares).
side_effects: Uric acid kidney stones; contraindicated in overproducers/stone history; can precipitate flare.
pearl: Inhibits tubular secretion of penicillin, raising penicillin levels. Avoid in stone formers; ensure good hydration. (High-dose aspirin antagonizes its uricosuric effect.)

name: Pegloticase
moa: Enzyme that converts uric acid to allantoin (highly water-soluble, easily excreted), rapidly lowering serum urate.
indications: Refractory chronic gout unresponsive to conventional urate-lowering therapy.
side_effects: Infusion reactions, anaphylaxis, gout flares; hemolysis/methemoglobinemia in G6PD deficiency.
pearl: Humans lack uricase; this IV enzyme supplies it. Screen for G6PD deficiency first (hydrogen peroxide byproduct risks hemolysis/methemoglobinemia).

name: Methotrexate
