moa: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase; anti-inflammatory effect at low weekly doses partly via increased adenosine release.
indications: First-line DMARD for rheumatoid arthritis; psoriasis, psoriatic arthritis; high-dose for chemotherapy.
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity, myelosuppression, mucositis/stomatitis, pulmonary fibrosis, teratogenic.
pearl: Dosed WEEKLY (not daily) for RA; supplement folic acid to reduce toxicity. Leucovorin rescue for overdose. Contraindicated in pregnancy.

name: Hydroxychloroquine
moa: Raises lysosomal/endosomal pH, impairing antigen processing and TLR signaling, dampening immune activation.
indications: Rheumatoid arthritis, SLE (esp. cutaneous/articular), malaria prophylaxis/treatment.
side_effects: Irreversible retinopathy (bull's-eye maculopathy), GI upset, QT prolongation.
pearl: Requires baseline and periodic eye exams for retinal toxicity. A cornerstone of SLE therapy.

name: Etanercept
drug_class: Biologic DMARD (TNF-alpha inhibitor; TNF receptor fusion protein)
moa: Soluble TNF-alpha receptor (p75)-Fc fusion decoy that binds and neutralizes circulating TNF-alpha.
indications: Rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, plaque psoriasis.
side_effects: Reactivation of latent TB and hepatitis B, serious infections, injection-site reactions.
pearl: Screen for latent TB (PPD/IGRA) before starting any TNF inhibitor. Etanercept is a fusion protein; infliximab/adalimumab are monoclonal antibodies.

name: Insulin lispro (also aspart, glulisine)
moa: Activates insulin receptor tyrosine kinase; drives glucose uptake (GLUT4) in muscle/fat, glycogen/lipid/protein synthesis; shifts K+ intracellularly
indications: Prandial (mealtime) coverage in T1DM and T2DM; insulin pumps
side_effects: Hypoglycemia, weight gain, lipodystrophy at injection sites, hypokalemia
pearl: Onset ~15 min — inject at/just before the meal. Amino-acid changes reduce hexamer self-association for faster absorption vs regular insulin.

name: Regular insulin
moa: Activates insulin receptor tyrosine kinase, promoting glucose uptake and anabolic effects; drives K+ into cells
indications: DKA and HHS (can be given IV), hyperkalemia, perioperative/inpatient management
side_effects: Hypoglycemia, hypokalemia (used therapeutically for hyperK with glucose), weight gain
pearl: Standard IV insulin for DKA. For hyperkalemia: give with glucose to drive K+ into cells while preventing hypoglycemia.

name: Insulin glargine (also detemir, degludec)
moa: Insulin receptor agonist; glargine precipitates at physiologic pH for slow, relatively peakless release giving steady basal levels
indications: Basal insulin coverage in T1DM and T2DM; once-daily dosing (glargine/degludec)
side_effects: Hypoglycemia (less nocturnal than NPH), weight gain
pearl: Relatively peakless — do NOT mix glargine in the same syringe with other insulins. Degludec has the longest duration (>42h).

name: Metformin
moa: Activates AMPK; inhibits hepatic gluconeogenesis (decreases glucose output), increases peripheral insulin sensitivity
indications: First-line for T2DM; also PCOS and prediabetes
side_effects: GI upset/diarrhea, B12 deficiency, lactic acidosis (rare)
pearl: Does NOT cause hypoglycemia or weight gain (weight-neutral/modest loss). Hold around iodinated contrast and in renal impairment (lactic acidosis risk).

name: Glyburide (also glipizide, glimepiride)
moa: Closes ATP-sensitive K+ channels on beta cells, depolarizing membrane, opening Ca2+ channels, triggering insulin release
indications: T2DM (requires functioning beta cells); add-on to metformin
side_effects: Hypoglycemia (esp. glyburide in elderly/renal), weight gain
pearl: Classic hypoglycemia risk. First-gen (tolbutamide, chlorpropamide) cause disulfiram-like reaction; chlorpropamide also causes SIADH.

name: Pioglitazone
moa: Agonist of nuclear PPAR-gamma; increases transcription of insulin-sensitive genes, improving peripheral insulin sensitivity (adipose/muscle)
indications: T2DM, especially with insulin resistance; add-on therapy
side_effects: Weight gain, fluid retention/edema, heart failure exacerbation, fractures, bladder cancer risk
pearl: Contraindicated in CHF (fluid retention). Slow onset — takes weeks. Rosiglitazone was restricted for CV concerns.

name: Sitagliptin
moa: Inhibits DPP-4, preventing breakdown of endogenous incretins (GLP-1, GIP); increases glucose-dependent insulin, lowers glucagon
indications: T2DM, oral add-on to metformin; weight-neutral option
side_effects: Generally well tolerated; nasopharyngitis, headache, pancreatitis (rare), arthralgia; urticaria/angioedema
pearl: Weight-neutral, low hypoglycemia risk. Saxagliptin/alogliptin linked to heart failure hospitalization signal.

name: Exenatide
moa: Activates GLP-1 receptors: glucose-dependent insulin secretion, suppresses glucagon, slows gastric emptying, increases satiety
indications: T2DM (esp. with obesity); injectable add-on
side_effects: Nausea/vomiting, pancreatitis, weight LOSS (often desired)
pearl: Promotes weight loss with low hypoglycemia risk. Contraindicated with personal/family history of medullary thyroid cancer or MEN2.

name: Semaglutide
moa: GLP-1 receptor agonist: glucose-dependent insulin release, decreased glucagon, delayed gastric emptying, central appetite suppression
indications: T2DM and chronic weight management (Wegovy); proven cardiovascular benefit
side_effects: Nausea/vomiting/diarrhea, pancreatitis, gallbladder disease, weight loss
pearl: Once-weekly injection; oral form exists. Contraindicated with medullary thyroid carcinoma/MEN2 (boxed warning). Strong CV and weight outcomes.

name: Empagliflozin (also canagliflozin, dapagliflozin)
moa: Blocks SGLT2 in proximal tubule, reducing glucose reabsorption and causing glucosuria; insulin-independent
indications: T2DM; heart failure (HFrEF & HFpEF); CKD — strong renal/cardiac protection
side_effects: Genital/urinary infections, volume depletion, euglycemic DKA, Fournier gangrene
pearl: Proven CV and renal benefit even in non-diabetics. Watch for euglycemic DKA — DKA with normal/near-normal glucose.

name: Acarbose (also miglitol)
moa: Inhibits intestinal brush-border alpha-glucosidases, delaying carbohydrate digestion and absorption; blunts postprandial glucose spike
indications: T2DM, targeting postprandial hyperglycemia; add-on therapy
side_effects: GI: flatulence, diarrhea, abdominal cramping (from carb malabsorption)
pearl: Does NOT cause hypoglycemia alone. If hypoglycemia occurs on combo therapy, treat with oral GLUCOSE — sucrose won't work (disaccharide breakdown blocked).

name: Pramlintide
moa: Synthetic analog of amylin; slows gastric emptying, suppresses glucagon secretion, increases satiety
indications: Adjunct to mealtime insulin in T1DM and T2DM
side_effects: Nausea, hypoglycemia (when combined with insulin), injection site reactions
pearl: Inject separately from insulin (different syringe). Amylin is co-secreted with insulin from beta cells normally; aids postprandial control.

name: Repaglinide (also nateglinide)
moa: Closes beta-cell ATP-sensitive K+ channels (binding site distinct from sulfonylureas), triggering rapid, short insulin release
indications: T2DM postprandial control; useful in sulfonylurea allergy and irregular meal timing
side_effects: Hypoglycemia (less than sulfonylureas), weight gain
pearl: Fast onset, short duration — take just before each meal; skip a meal, skip the dose. Lower hypoglycemia risk than sulfonylureas.

name: Glucagon
moa: Activates hepatic glucagon receptors (Gs/cAMP): stimulates glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis to raise blood glucose
indications: Severe hypoglycemia when patient can't take oral glucose; beta-blocker/CCB overdose
side_effects: Nausea, vomiting, hyperglycemia, hypokalemia
pearl: Antidote for severe insulin-induced hypoglycemia in an unconscious patient. Ineffective if glycogen stores depleted (alcoholics, prolonged fasting).

name: Levothyroxine (T4)
moa: Synthetic thyroxine (T4); peripherally deiodinated to active T3, which binds nuclear thyroid receptors to regulate gene transcription and metabolic rate.
indications: Hypothyroidism (1st-line replacement), myxedema coma, post-thyroidectomy, TSH suppression for goiter/thyroid cancer.
side_effects: Iatrogenic thyrotoxicosis if overdosed: tachycardia/AFib, weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor, osteoporosis, insomnia.
pearl: Take on empty stomach; separate from Ca/Fe/PPIs (impair absorption). Increase dose in pregnancy. Monitor TSH (6-8 wks after change).

name: Liothyronine (T3)
moa: Synthetic triiodothyronine; directly binds nuclear thyroid receptors; faster onset and shorter half-life than T4.
indications: Myxedema coma (rapid action), select hypothyroidism, T3 suppression testing.
side_effects: Greater cardiac toxicity than T4: arrhythmias, angina, palpitations, tremor.
pearl: Not preferred for chronic replacement (short half-life causes peaks/troughs); higher cardiac risk than levothyroxine.

name: Propylthiouracil (PTU)
moa: Inhibits thyroid peroxidase (blocks iodine organification/coupling) AND blocks peripheral 5'-deiodinase conversion of T4 to T3.
indications: Hyperthyroidism/Graves, thyroid storm; preferred in 1st trimester pregnancy.
side_effects: Agranulocytosis, severe hepatotoxicity/fulminant hepatic failure, rash, ANCA-associated vasculitis.
pearl: DOC in 1st trimester (methimazole = teratogen: aplasia cutis). Black-box hepatotoxicity. Blocks peripheral T4->T3 (useful in storm).

name: Methimazole
moa: Inhibits thyroid peroxidase, blocking iodine organification and coupling of iodotyrosines; no peripheral conversion effect.
indications: Hyperthyroidism/Graves (1st-line), pre-thyroidectomy/RAI prep, thyroid storm (after 1st trimester).
side_effects: Agranulocytosis (most feared), rash, cholestasis, teratogenic (aplasia cutis, choanal/esophageal atresia).
pearl: More potent, longer-acting, less hepatotoxic than PTU. AVOID 1st trimester (teratogen). Check CBC if fever/sore throat (agranulocytosis).

name: Potassium iodide (Lugol's/SSKI)
moa: High-dose iodide transiently inhibits hormone release and organification (Wolff-Chaikoff effect); reduces gland vascularity.
indications: Thyroid storm, preoperative thyroidectomy prep, radiation protection (blocks I-131 thyroid uptake).
side_effects: Sialadenitis, metallic taste, iodism, hypersensitivity; can worsen/cause hyperthyroidism (Jod-Basedow) with escape.
pearl: Give >=1 hr AFTER thioamide in storm to avoid fueling hormone synthesis. Effect transient (escape from Wolff-Chaikoff).

name: Hydrocortisone
moa: Binds cytosolic glucocorticoid receptor; alters gene transcription, induces lipocortin/annexin-1 inhibiting phospholipase A2, suppresses NF-kB.
indications: Adrenal insufficiency/Addisonian crisis replacement, septic shock, anti-inflammatory.
side_effects: Cushingoid features, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, immunosuppression, HPA suppression, fluid retention/hypertension.
pearl: Glucocorticoid replacement of choice in adrenal insufficiency (short-acting, mimics cortisol); primary (Addison) AI also needs added fludrocortisone for mineralocorticoid replacement. Stress-dose for surgery/illness.

name: Prednisone
moa: Prodrug converted to active prednisolone in liver; activates glucocorticoid receptor for anti-inflammatory/immunosuppressive gene regulation.
indications: Asthma/COPD exacerbation, autoimmune disease, transplant immunosuppression, allergy, inflammatory conditions.
side_effects: Hyperglycemia, weight gain, osteoporosis, cataracts/glaucoma, peptic ulcer, mood changes, infection, growth suppression.
pearl: Never stop abruptly after chronic use - taper to avoid adrenal crisis (HPA suppression). Liver-activated prodrug (avoid in severe liver dz).

name: Dexamethasone
moa: Highly potent glucocorticoid-receptor agonist; long duration of action, minimal sodium/water retention.
indications: Cerebral edema, croup, CINV, ARDS/severe COVID, fetal lung maturity, dexamethasone suppression test.
side_effects: Glucocorticoid class effects: hyperglycemia, immunosuppression, psychosis, HPA suppression, osteoporosis.
pearl: Used in dexamethasone suppression test for Cushing. No mineralocorticoid activity. Crosses placenta (betamethasone/dex for fetal lungs).

name: Fludrocortisone
moa: Potent mineralocorticoid-receptor agonist; promotes renal Na+/water retention and K+/H+ excretion in distal nephron.
indications: Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison) mineralocorticoid replacement, orthostatic hypotension.
side_effects: Hypertension, edema, hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, heart failure exacerbation.
pearl: Added to hydrocortisone in primary AI (not needed in secondary AI since aldosterone is RAAS-driven). Monitor K+ and BP.

name: Ketoconazole
moa: Inhibits multiple CYP450 steroidogenic enzymes (desmolase, 17,20-lyase, 11-beta-hydroxylase) - blocks cortisol & androgen synthesis.
indications: Cushing syndrome (medical control of hypercortisolism), inoperable adrenal/ectopic ACTH tumors.
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity (black box), gynecomastia, decreased libido, adrenal insufficiency, QT prolongation.
pearl: Blocks adrenal AND gonadal steroid synthesis -> gynecomastia. Strong CYP3A4 inhibitor. Hepatotoxicity limits use.

name: Metyrapone
moa: Inhibits 11-beta-hydroxylase, blocking conversion of 11-deoxycortisol to cortisol; 11-deoxycortisol accumulates.
indications: Cushing syndrome (medical therapy), diagnostic testing of HPA-axis/ACTH reserve.
side_effects: Hypertension/hirsutism (11-deoxycorticosterone & androgen precursor buildup), nausea, dizziness, adrenal insufficiency.
pearl: Metyrapone test: blocks cortisol -> normal pituitary raises ACTH and urinary 11-deoxycortisol metabolite; assesses HPA reserve.

name: Octreotide
moa: Long-acting somatostatin analog; inhibits secretion of GH, glucagon, insulin, gastrin, serotonin, VIP, and other GI hormones.
indications: Acromegaly, carcinoid syndrome/VIPoma, esophageal variceal/GI bleeding, severe secretory diarrhea.
side_effects: Gallstones/biliary sludge, steatorrhea, hyperglycemia or hypoglycemia, bradycardia, abdominal cramps.
pearl: Cholelithiasis (inhibited gallbladder contraction) is the classic adverse effect. Medical Rx for acromegaly (esp. post-surgery).

name: Desmopressin (DDAVP)
moa: Selective V2-receptor agonist; increases aquaporin-2 insertion in collecting duct (water reabsorption); raises vWF/factor VIII.
indications: Central diabetes insipidus, nocturnal enuresis, von Willebrand disease/mild hemophilia A.
side_effects: Hyponatremia/water intoxication, seizures, headache, facial flushing.
pearl: Water deprivation test: corrects urine osmolality in central DI, not nephrogenic DI. Releases vWF (hemostasis use).

name: Cinacalcet
moa: Allosterically activates the calcium-sensing receptor on parathyroid cells, increasing sensitivity to Ca2+ -> decreased PTH secretion.
indications: Secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD/dialysis, parathyroid carcinoma, primary hyperparathyroidism (inoperable).
side_effects: Hypocalcemia (can cause QT prolongation/seizures), nausea/vomiting, hypotension.
pearl: Lowers both PTH and serum calcium. Monitor calcium; hypocalcemia is the key toxicity. Contraindicated if baseline hypocalcemia.

name: Spironolactone
moa: Competitively blocks aldosterone receptor in distal nephron (K+-sparing diuresis); also antiandrogen (blocks androgen receptor).
indications: Primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn), heart failure (reduces mortality), ascites/cirrhosis, PCOS/hirsutism, acne.
side_effects: Hyperkalemia, gynecomastia, menstrual irregularity, antiandrogen effects, metabolic acidosis.
pearl: Antiandrogen activity -> gynecomastia (eplerenone is selective, spares this). Watch K+, especially with ACEi/ARB.

name: Omeprazole
moa: Irreversibly inhibits H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump) on gastric parietal cells; prodrug activated in acidic canaliculi.
indications: GERD, peptic ulcer disease, H. pylori (triple therapy), Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, erosive esophagitis.
side_effects: Hypomagnesemia, B12/iron/calcium malabsorption, increased fracture risk, C. difficile colitis, pneumonia, acute interstitial nephritis.
pearl: Inhibits CYP2C19 -> reduces clopidogrel activation. Take 30-60 min before meals. Suffix '-prazole'.

name: Pantoprazole
moa: Irreversibly inhibits parietal cell H+/K+ ATPase; prodrug activated in acidic environment.
indications: GERD, erosive esophagitis, PUD, GI bleed (IV), preferred PPI when drug interactions are a concern.
side_effects: Hypomagnesemia, increased C. difficile risk, B12 deficiency (chronic use), interstitial nephritis.
pearl: Least CYP450 interaction of the PPIs -> favored with clopidogrel and in ICU IV use.

name: Ranitidine
moa: Reversibly blocks histamine H2 receptors on parietal cells -> reduced cAMP -> decreased gastric acid secretion.
indications: GERD, PUD, mild dyspepsia, nocturnal acid suppression.
side_effects: Generally well tolerated; headache, thrombocytopenia, confusion in elderly/renal impairment.
pearl: Withdrawn from market due to NDMA (carcinogen) contamination. Suffix '-tidine'.

name: Cimetidine
moa: Reversibly blocks parietal cell H2 receptors -> decreased histamine-mediated gastric acid secretion.
indications: GERD, PUD, dyspepsia (largely supplanted by safer agents/PPIs).
side_effects: Antiandrogen effects: gynecomastia, impotence, decreased libido; CNS confusion in elderly; increased prolactin.
pearl: Potent CYP450 inhibitor (many drug interactions) + antiandrogenic effects distinguish it from other H2 blockers.

name: Famotidine
moa: Reversibly blocks parietal cell histamine H2 receptors -> decreased gastric acid secretion.
indications: GERD, PUD, dyspepsia; preferred H2 blocker (no antiandrogen or major CYP effects).
side_effects: Headache, dizziness; confusion with renal impairment; very low interaction profile.
pearl: No CYP450 inhibition and no antiandrogenic effects -> safest/most-used H2 blocker (contrast with cimetidine).

name: Ondansetron
moa: Blocks serotonin 5-HT3 receptors centrally (chemoreceptor trigger zone/area postrema) and on vagal afferents in the gut.
indications: Chemo-induced (CINV), post-op, and radiation-induced nausea/vomiting; hyperemesis.
side_effects: QT prolongation, headache, constipation; serotonin syndrome risk in combination.
pearl: Dose-dependent QT prolongation -> get baseline ECG/electrolytes. Suffix '-setron'.

name: Metoclopramide
moa: Blocks central D2 receptors (antiemetic) and peripherally increases ACh release -> increased gastric motility and LES tone; mild 5-HT4 agonism.
indications: Diabetic gastroparesis, GERD, antiemetic, postop ileus.
side_effects: Extrapyramidal symptoms (acute dystonia, tardive dyskinesia), hyperprolactinemia (galactorrhea), NMS, drowsiness.
pearl: Tardive dyskinesia carries a black box warning; contraindicated in bowel obstruction and Parkinson disease.

name: Promethazine
moa: Blocks H1 histamine, D2 dopamine, and muscarinic receptors centrally to suppress nausea/vomiting.
indications: Nausea/vomiting, motion sickness, allergic reactions, sedation.
side_effects: Sedation, anticholinergic effects, extrapyramidal symptoms; severe tissue injury/gangrene if IV extravasation.
pearl: Black box: respiratory depression in children <2 yr; do not give to young kids.

name: Aprepitant
moa: Blocks substance P binding at central NK1 receptors in the brainstem vomiting center.
indications: Prevention of delayed and acute chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting (with 5-HT3 antagonist + dexamethasone).
side_effects: Fatigue, hiccups, diarrhea; CYP3A4 interactions.
pearl: Targets the DELAYED phase of CINV; moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor (affects dexamethasone, warfarin, OCPs).

name: Scopolamine
moa: Antagonizes muscarinic ACh receptors in the vestibular system and vomiting center.
indications: Motion sickness (transdermal patch), postoperative nausea/vomiting.
side_effects: Anticholinergic: dry mouth, blurred vision, mydriasis, urinary retention, confusion (esp. elderly).
pearl: Transdermal patch behind the ear is classic for motion sickness; first-line for prevention.

name: Psyllium
moa: Indigestible hydrophilic fiber absorbs water, swells, and increases stool bulk -> stimulates peristalsis.
indications: Chronic constipation, IBS, diverticulosis; cholesterol lowering.
side_effects: Bloating, flatulence, abdominal cramping; bowel obstruction if inadequate fluid intake.
pearl: Must be taken with plenty of water; safest laxative for chronic use and in pregnancy.

name: Polyethylene glycol
moa: Osmotically active, non-absorbable agent that draws water into the bowel lumen -> softer stool, increased motility.
indications: Chronic constipation; high-volume prep for bowel/colonoscopy cleansing.
side_effects: Bloating, cramping, nausea; electrolyte disturbances mainly with large prep volumes.
pearl: PEG (MiraLAX) is preferred first-line osmotic for chronic constipation; does not cause significant electrolyte shifts at normal doses.

name: Lactulose
moa: Gut bacteria metabolize it to organic acids -> osmotic water draw AND acidification traps NH3 as NH4+ for excretion.
indications: Constipation; hepatic encephalopathy (lowers blood ammonia).
side_effects: Bloating, flatulence, cramps, diarrhea; dehydration/hypernatremia with overuse.
pearl: Key drug for hepatic encephalopathy -> titrate to 2-3 soft stools/day to lower ammonia.

name: Loperamide
moa: Activates mu-opioid receptors in the myenteric plexus -> slows gut motility, increases transit time and fluid absorption.
indications: Acute and chronic non-infectious diarrhea, ileostomy output reduction.
side_effects: Constipation, abdominal cramps; high-dose abuse -> QT prolongation, torsades, cardiac arrest.
pearl: Does not cross BBB at therapeutic doses (no CNS effects/euphoria); avoid in bloody/febrile (invasive) diarrhea -> toxic megacolon risk.

name: Albuterol
moa: Selective beta-2 adrenergic agonist; activates adenylyl cyclase, raises cAMP, relaxes bronchial smooth muscle for rapid bronchodilation.
indications: Acute asthma/bronchospasm rescue, COPD exacerbations, exercise-induced bronchospasm.
side_effects: Tremor, tachycardia/palpitations, hypokalemia (K+ shifts intracellularly), hyperglycemia, anxiety.
pearl: First-line acute reliever; rising SABA use signals poor control. Also shifts K+ intracellularly to treat hyperkalemia.

name: Salmeterol
moa: Highly lipophilic long-acting beta-2 agonist; sustained cAMP-mediated bronchodilation. Slow onset (~15-30 min), so NOT for rescue.
indications: Asthma maintenance (only WITH an ICS) and COPD long-term control.
side_effects: Tremor, tachycardia, hypokalemia; QT prolongation; tachyphylaxis with chronic use.
pearl: LABA monotherapy in asthma raises asthma-related death (historic black box) — must pair with an ICS. Slow onset, not a rescue inhaler.

name: Formoterol
moa: Long-acting beta-2 agonist with rapid onset (~1-3 min); cAMP-mediated bronchodilation.
indications: Asthma/COPD maintenance; component of SMART/MART regimen (ICS-formoterol as controller and reliever).
side_effects: Tremor, tachycardia, hypokalemia, headache; QT prolongation.
pearl: Unlike salmeterol, has rapid onset, so ICS-formoterol can serve as both maintenance AND reliever (MART/SMART). Still must combine with ICS in asthma.

name: Tiotropium
moa: Long-acting muscarinic antagonist; blocks ACh-mediated bronchoconstriction. Long duration from slow dissociation, esp. at M3.
indications: COPD maintenance (first-line); add-on for severe/uncontrolled asthma.
side_effects: Dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision; caution in narrow-angle glaucoma/BPH.
pearl: Mainstay COPD maintenance bronchodilator. Anticholinergic — caution in glaucoma and prostatic hyperplasia.

name: Ipratropium
moa: Non-selective short-acting muscarinic antagonist; blocks ACh at bronchial M receptors. Quaternary amine — poorly absorbed systemically.
indications: Acute COPD exacerbations (with SABA), acute severe asthma adjunct.
side_effects: Dry mouth, cough; minimal systemic effects (quaternary, poorly absorbed).
pearl: Atropine derivative but quaternary, so few systemic anticholinergic effects. Key in COPD; with albuterol = DuoNeb.

name: Fluticasone
moa: Binds glucocorticoid receptor, alters gene transcription to suppress airway inflammation/cytokines and reduce bronchial hyperresponsiveness.
indications: First-line maintenance controller for persistent asthma; COPD with frequent exacerbations.
side_effects: Oral candidiasis (thrush), dysphonia/hoarseness; high-dose: adrenal suppression, reduced growth velocity in kids, osteoporosis.
pearl: Rinse mouth and use a spacer to prevent thrush. Controller, not reliever — most important long-term asthma medication.

name: Budesonide
moa: Glucocorticoid receptor agonist; anti-inflammatory via reduced cytokine production and airway inflammation.
indications: Persistent asthma maintenance; pairs with formoterol for SMART; nebulized form used in young children.
side_effects: Oral thrush, dysphonia; systemic effects (adrenal suppression) at high dose.
pearl: Preferred ICS in pregnancy (most safety data). Budesonide-formoterol is the classic single-inhaler maintenance-and-reliever combo.

name: Montelukast
moa: Blocks the CysLT1 receptor, antagonizing leukotrienes D4/E4 to reduce bronchoconstriction, inflammation, and mucus.
indications: Asthma maintenance (esp. aspirin-exacerbated and exercise-induced), allergic rhinitis. Oral, once daily.
side_effects: Headache; neuropsychiatric effects — agitation, depression, sleep disturbance, suicidal ideation (FDA boxed warning).
pearl: Especially useful in aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (Samter triad) and exercise-induced bronchospasm. Boxed warning for neuropsych effects.

name: Zileuton
moa: Inhibits 5-lipoxygenase, blocking synthesis of all leukotrienes (LTB4 and cysteinyl LTs) from arachidonic acid.
indications: Chronic asthma maintenance (second/third line).
side_effects: Hepatotoxicity — monitor LFTs; headache. CYP1A2 inhibitor, raising theophylline, warfarin, and propranolol levels.
pearl: Blocks leukotriene SYNTHESIS (vs montelukast blocking the receptor). Hepatotoxic — requires liver enzyme monitoring.

name: Theophylline
moa: Nonselective phosphodiesterase inhibitor (raises cAMP) and adenosine receptor antagonist; bronchodilation plus mild anti-inflammatory and respiratory-drive effects.
indications: Refractory asthma/COPD (rarely used now due to toxicity).
side_effects: Narrow therapeutic index: nausea, insomnia, tremor; toxicity causes seizures and cardiac arrhythmias (can be fatal).
pearl: Narrow therapeutic window; metabolized by CYP1A2 — many interactions (cimetidine, ciprofloxacin, zileuton raise levels). Toxicity = seizures/arrhythmias.

name: Omalizumab
moa: Anti-IgE mAb; binds free IgE, preventing it from engaging FceRI on mast cells/basophils, dampening the allergic cascade.
indications: Moderate-severe allergic asthma uncontrolled on ICS; chronic spontaneous urticaria; nasal polyps.
side_effects: Injection-site reactions; anaphylaxis (boxed warning — observe after dosing).
pearl: For severe allergic asthma with elevated IgE; dosed by weight and IgE level. Boxed warning for anaphylaxis. Prototype type 2/allergic asthma biologic.

name: Diphenhydramine
moa: Reversible H1 inverse agonist/antagonist; crosses the blood-brain barrier and has anticholinergic activity.
indications: Allergic rhinitis, urticaria, acute allergic reactions, motion sickness, sleep aid, antiemetic.
side_effects: Sedation, anticholinergic effects (dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision), confusion in elderly.
pearl: Sedating + anticholinergic from CNS penetration. On Beers list — avoid in elderly. Overdose mimics atropine (anticholinergic) toxicity.

name: Loratadine
moa: Selective peripheral H1 antagonist; poor CNS penetration (P-glycoprotein substrate) so minimally sedating.
indications: Allergic rhinitis, chronic urticaria; first-line for daily allergy symptom control.
side_effects: Minimal sedation; little to no anticholinergic effect; headache.
pearl: Preferred over 1st-gen for daily control — non-sedating, low anticholinergic burden. Among 2nd-gen, cetirizine is the most sedating.

name: Roflumilast
moa: Selectively inhibits phosphodiesterase-4, raising cAMP in inflammatory cells to reduce airway inflammation; anti-inflammatory, not a bronchodilator.
indications: Severe COPD with chronic bronchitis and frequent exacerbations (add-on).
side_effects: Diarrhea, nausea, weight loss, headache; neuropsychiatric effects (depression, suicidality).
pearl: Reduces exacerbations in the chronic-bronchitis COPD phenotype. Anti-inflammatory, NOT a bronchodilator — distinguishes it from theophylline.
